Optimal Transport for Machine Learners

Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Entropic Regularization: Convergence

Convergence for entropic optimal transport has two complementary meanings. At fixed marginals and fixed temperature, one studies how Sinkhorn iterates approach the regularized optimizer. When the marginals are empirical, one also studies how the regularized value and potentials behave as the number of samples grows toward a mean-field limit.

This chapter keeps these two scales together. It starts with algorithmic convergence of matrix scaling and soft transforms, then explains the Hilbert metric contraction mechanism, and finally turns to Gaussian closed forms, sample-complexity consequences, and a non-variational M-function viewpoint. The recurring message is that entropy helps optimization and statistics by smoothing the problem, but the constants deteriorate as the temperature ϵ\epsilonϵ goes to zero.

Guiding Comparison

There are three useful convergence stories for Sinkhorn. Bregman projections explain the geometry of alternating row and column corrections; Fortet’s monotone argument explains qualitative fixed-point convergence of soft transforms; Hilbert’s projective metric gives a linear contraction when the kernel is uniformly positive. Statistical convergence is a different story: fixed-temperature Sinkhorn behaves like a smooth discrepancy, while exact OT keeps its dimension-dependent empirical rates. A fourth, more algebraic story views Sinkhorn as a monotone coordinate-clearing system, which survives beyond variational OT.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Sinkhorn Convergence: Bregman View¶

Sinkhorn can be read as alternating Bregman projections. The main geometric message is simple: each row or column rescaling is the KL projection onto one affine marginal constraint. The convergence mechanism then follows from the Pythagorean identity for Bregman divergences.

For simplicity, this section is written for discrete measures. The same ideas carry over to general measures, and the robust-rate section below expresses the constants through cost and potential oscillations rather than through the number of grid points.

Alternating KL Projections¶

The projection viewpoint explains Sinkhorn as repeated enforcement of one marginal constraint at a time. It is not specific to entropy, although KL is the case where the projections reduce to elementary row and column scalings.

Definition: Bregman Divergence

Let Φ\PhiΦ be a differentiable strictly convex function on a convex domain Ω\OmegaΩ. The Bregman divergence generated by Φ\PhiΦ is

BΦ(P∣Q):=Φ(P)−Φ(Q)−⟨∇Φ(Q),P−Q⟩.B_\Phi(P\mid Q) \eqdef \Phi(P)-\Phi(Q)-\langle \nabla\Phi(Q),P-Q\rangle .BΦ​(P∣Q):=Φ(P)−Φ(Q)−⟨∇Φ(Q),P−Q⟩.
(1)

For the negative entropy Φ(P)=∑i,jPi,jlog⁡Pi,j\Phi(P)=\sum_{i,j}P_{i,j}\log P_{i,j}Φ(P)=∑i,j​Pi,j​logPi,j​ on the positive orthant, one obtains BΦ(P∣Q)=KL⁡(P∣Q)B_\Phi(P\mid Q)=\operatorname{KL}(P\mid Q)BΦ​(P∣Q)=KL(P∣Q), up to the harmless convention at the boundary.

Bregman divergences are useful because their geometry can encode constraints. A Legendre-type generator blows up, or has an infinite derivative, at the boundary of its domain. For negative entropy, positivity is therefore built into the divergence, so one projects onto affine marginal constraints without separately handling non-negativity.

Linear Tilts and Gibbs References¶

Adding a linear cost to a Bregman penalty merely shifts the reference point in dual coordinates. The usual Gibbs--KL reformulation is exactly the entropy specialization.

Proposition: Linear Tilts of Bregman Penalties

Let Φ\PhiΦ be differentiable and strictly convex, and let BΦB_\PhiBΦ​ be its Bregman divergence. Fix a reference point QQQ in the interior of the domain. Assume that there exists QCQ^CQC such that

∇Φ(QC)=∇Φ(Q)−C/ϵ.\nabla\Phi(Q^C)=\nabla\Phi(Q)-C/\epsilon .∇Φ(QC)=∇Φ(Q)−C/ϵ.
(2)

Then, for all PPP in the domain,

⟨P,C⟩+ϵBΦ(P∣Q)=ϵBΦ(P∣QC)+cst,\langle P,C\rangle+\epsilon B_\Phi(P\mid Q) = \epsilon B_\Phi(P\mid Q^C)+\text{cst},⟨P,C⟩+ϵBΦ​(P∣Q)=ϵBΦ​(P∣QC)+cst,
(3)

where the constant does not depend on PPP.

Proof

Subtract the two Bregman divergences:

BΦ(P∣QC)−BΦ(P∣Q)=⟨∇Φ(Q)−∇Φ(QC),P⟩+cst.B_\Phi(P\mid Q^C)-B_\Phi(P\mid Q) = \langle \nabla\Phi(Q)-\nabla\Phi(Q^C),P\rangle+\text{cst}.BΦ​(P∣QC)−BΦ​(P∣Q)=⟨∇Φ(Q)−∇Φ(QC),P⟩+cst.
(4)

Using ∇Φ(Q)−∇Φ(QC)=C/ϵ\nabla\Phi(Q)-\nabla\Phi(Q^C)=C/\epsilon∇Φ(Q)−∇Φ(QC)=C/ϵ and multiplying by ϵ\epsilonϵ gives the claim.

For the negative entropy, take Q=a⊗bQ=a\otimes bQ=a⊗b. The tilted reference is

Ka,bϵ:=(a⊗b)⊙e−C/ϵ.K_{a,b}^\epsilon \eqdef (a\otimes b)\odot e^{-C/\epsilon}.Ka,bϵ​:=(a⊗b)⊙e−C/ϵ.
(5)

Thus

⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL⁡(P∣a⊗b)=ϵKL⁡(P∣Ka,bϵ)+cst.\langle P,C\rangle+\epsilon\operatorname{KL}(P\mid a\otimes b) = \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(P\mid K_{a,b}^\epsilon)+\text{cst}.⟨P,C⟩+ϵKL(P∣a⊗b)=ϵKL(P∣Ka,bϵ​)+cst.
(6)

On the transport polytope, scaling Ka,bϵK_{a,b}^\epsilonKa,bϵ​ is equivalent to scaling the Gibbs kernel K=e−C/ϵK=e^{-C/\epsilon}K=e−C/ϵ because the factors aia_iai​ and bjb_jbj​ can be absorbed into the Sinkhorn scalings. The unique entropic optimizer is the KL projection of this tilted Gibbs reference onto the coupling constraints:

Pϵ=arg min⁡P∈U(a,b)KL⁡(P∣Ka,bϵ).P_\epsilon = \argmin_{P\in\mathcal U(a,b)} \operatorname{KL}(P\mid K_{a,b}^\epsilon).Pϵ​=P∈U(a,b)argmin​KL(P∣Ka,bϵ​).
(7)

Cyclic Projection Convergence¶

The convergence mechanism is the classical one of Bregman projections Bregman, 1967.

Proposition: Cyclic Bregman Projections on Affine Constraints

Let Φ\PhiΦ be a Legendre strictly convex generator on a finite-dimensional convex domain, and let C1,C2\mathcal C_1,\mathcal C_2C1​,C2​ be affine constraint sets whose intersection meets the domain. Define

Pk+1=Proj⁡C2BΦProj⁡C1BΦ(Pk),P_{k+1} = \operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_2}^{B_\Phi} \operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_1}^{B_\Phi}(P_k),Pk+1​=ProjC2​BΦ​​ProjC1​BΦ​​(Pk​),
(8)

starting from an interior point P0P_0P0​. Assume the projections are well-defined and the iterates remain in a compact subset of the domain. Then PkP_kPk​ converges to the Bregman projection of P0P_0P0​ onto C1∩C2\mathcal C_1\cap\mathcal C_2C1​∩C2​. In particular, the KL case converges for positive affine marginal constraints on a bounded transportation polytope.

Proof Sketch

For three interior points one has the Bregman three-point formula

BΦ(Q∣P)=BΦ(Q∣P+)+BΦ(P+∣P)+⟨∇Φ(P+)−∇Φ(P),Q−P+⟩.B_\Phi(Q\mid P) = B_\Phi(Q\mid P^+) + B_\Phi(P^+\mid P) + \langle\nabla\Phi(P^+)-\nabla\Phi(P),Q-P^+\rangle .BΦ​(Q∣P)=BΦ​(Q∣P+)+BΦ​(P+∣P)+⟨∇Φ(P+)−∇Φ(P),Q−P+⟩.
(9)

If P+=Proj⁡CBΦ(P)P^+=\operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C}^{B_\Phi}(P)P+=ProjCBΦ​​(P) and C\mathcal CC is affine, the first-order condition cancels the last term for all Q∈CQ\in\mathcal CQ∈C. This gives the Pythagorean identity

BΦ(Q∣P)=BΦ(Q∣P+)+BΦ(P+∣P).B_\Phi(Q\mid P) = B_\Phi(Q\mid P^+)+B_\Phi(P^+\mid P).BΦ​(Q∣P)=BΦ​(Q∣P+)+BΦ​(P+∣P).
(10)

Applying this identity at every half-step shows that BΦ(Q∣Zℓ)B_\Phi(Q\mid Z_\ell)BΦ​(Q∣Zℓ​) decreases for every Q∈C1∩C2Q\in\mathcal C_1\cap\mathcal C_2Q∈C1​∩C2​, and that the projection drops are summable. Compactness gives cluster points. The adjacent half-steps approach one another, so every cluster point belongs to both affine sets. The telescoped first-order conditions give the normal-cone optimality condition for the Bregman projection onto the intersection. Strict convexity gives uniqueness, hence the whole sequence converges.

Let

Ca1:={P:P1m=a},Cb2:={P:P⊤1n=b}.\mathcal C_a^1 \eqdef \{P:P\mathbf 1_m=a\}, \qquad \mathcal C_b^2 \eqdef \{P:P^\top\mathbf 1_n=b\}.Ca1​:={P:P1m​=a},Cb2​:={P:P⊤1n​=b}.
(11)

Then U(a,b)=Ca1∩Cb2\mathcal U(a,b)=\mathcal C_a^1\cap\mathcal C_b^2U(a,b)=Ca1​∩Cb2​, and KL cyclic projections read

P(2ℓ+1)=Proj⁡Ca1KL⁡(P(2ℓ)),P(2ℓ+2)=Proj⁡Cb2KL⁡(P(2ℓ+1)).P^{(2\ell+1)} = \operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_a^1}^{\operatorname{KL}}(P^{(2\ell)}), \qquad P^{(2\ell+2)} = \operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_b^2}^{\operatorname{KL}}(P^{(2\ell+1)}).P(2ℓ+1)=ProjCa1​KL​(P(2ℓ)),P(2ℓ+2)=ProjCb2​KL​(P(2ℓ+1)).
(12)

The preceding proposition applies with P0=Ka,bϵP_0=K_{a,b}^\epsilonP0​=Ka,bϵ​ and gives convergence to the entropic optimizer.

Row and Column Scalings¶

The projectors are explicit because they only rescale rows or columns.

Proposition: KL Projections are Scalings

One has

Proj⁡Ca1KL⁡(P)=diag⁡ ⁣(aP1m)P,Proj⁡Cb2KL⁡(P)=Pdiag⁡ ⁣(bP⊤1n).\operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_a^1}^{\operatorname{KL}}(P) = \operatorname{diag}\!\left(\frac{a}{P\mathbf 1_m}\right)P, \qquad \operatorname{Proj}_{\mathcal C_b^2}^{\operatorname{KL}}(P) = P\operatorname{diag}\!\left(\frac{b}{P^\top\mathbf 1_n}\right).ProjCa1​KL​(P)=diag(P1m​a​)P,ProjCb2​KL​(P)=Pdiag(P⊤1n​b​).
(13)
Proof

Along each row or column vector, impose a fixed sum s>0s>0s>0:

min⁡p{KL⁡(p∣q):⟨p,1⟩=s}.\min_p \left\{ \operatorname{KL}(p\mid q) : \langle p,\mathbf 1\rangle=s \right\}.pmin​{KL(p∣q):⟨p,1⟩=s}.
(14)

The Lagrange multiplier equation gives log⁡(p/q)+λ1=0\log(p/q)+\lambda\mathbf 1=0log(p/q)+λ1=0, hence p=uqp=uqp=uq with u=e−λ>0u=e^{-\lambda}>0u=e−λ>0. The constraint fixes u=s/∑iqiu=s/\sum_i q_iu=s/∑i​qi​, which is exactly the scaling formula.

Defining

P(2ℓ):=diag⁡(u(ℓ))Kdiag⁡(v(ℓ)),P^{(2\ell)} \eqdef \operatorname{diag}(u^{(\ell)})K\operatorname{diag}(v^{(\ell)}),P(2ℓ):=diag(u(ℓ))Kdiag(v(ℓ)),
(15)

the two projection steps are the usual Sinkhorn updates on the scaling vectors. In practice one stores the vectors and multiplies by the Gibbs kernel, often exploiting separable, sparse, low-rank or geometric structure.

The Bregman proof is geometric, but its direct finite-dimensional linear-rate constants can degrade with dimension and with small ϵ\epsilonϵ. The robust dual analysis below gives a dimension-free qualitative message: before any local linear regime becomes visible, one can still guarantee an O(1/k)O(1/k)O(1/k) dual gap whose constants depend on the cost range and potential oscillation.

Algorithm: Cyclic Bregman projections

Input: Constraint sets C1,C2\Cc_1,\Cc_2C1​,C2​, Bregman divergence BΦB_\PhiBΦ​, interior point P0P_0P0​, constraint defects defC1,defC2\mathrm{def}_{\Cc_1},\mathrm{def}_{\Cc_2}defC1​​,defC2​​, tolerance tol\mathrm{tol}tol.

Output: Point in C1∩C2\Cc_1\cap\Cc_2C1​∩C2​ when the intersection is feasible.

Specialize to entropic OT, if needed: C1=Ca1,C2=Cb2,BΦ=KL⁡.\Cc_1=\Cc^1_\a, \qquad \Cc_2=\Cc^2_\b, \qquad B_\Phi=\KL.C1​=Ca1​,C2​=Cb2​,BΦ​=KL.

Initialize: Set r0=+∞r_0=+\inftyr0​=+∞ and k=0k=0k=0.

While rk>tolr_k>\mathrm{tol}rk​>tol do:

Set k←k+1k\leftarrow k+1k←k+1.

Pk−1/2=Proj⁡C1BΦ(Pk−1).P_{k-1/2}=\Proj_{\Cc_1}^{B_\Phi}(P_{k-1}).Pk−1/2​=ProjC1​BΦ​​(Pk−1​).

Pk=Proj⁡C2BΦ(Pk−1/2).P_k=\Proj_{\Cc_2}^{B_\Phi}(P_{k-1/2}).Pk​=ProjC2​BΦ​​(Pk−1/2​).

Set rk=max⁡{defC1(Pk),defC2(Pk)}r_k=\max\{\mathrm{def}_{\Cc_1}(P_k),\mathrm{def}_{\Cc_2}(P_k)\}rk​=max{defC1​​(Pk​),defC2​​(Pk​)}.

Return PkP_kPk​.

Sinkhorn Convergence: Monotone Point of View¶

There is another, older route to convergence, going back to Fortet’s proof of the Schrodinger system Fortet, 1940Essid & Pavon, 2019Léonard, 2019. It uses the order structure of soft transforms rather than an explicit contraction factor.

Proposition: Monotone Fixed-Point Route to Sinkhorn Convergence

Let ccc be bounded and continuous on compact spaces, and define the double Sinkhorn map, normalized by subtracting its α\alphaα-mean,

A(f):=(fc,ϵ)cˉ,ϵ−∫(fc,ϵ)cˉ,ϵ  dα.\mathcal A(f) \eqdef (f^{c,\epsilon})^{\bar c,\epsilon} - \int (f^{c,\epsilon})^{\bar c,\epsilon}\,\d\alpha .A(f):=(fc,ϵ)cˉ,ϵ−∫(fc,ϵ)cˉ,ϵdα.
(16)

The map A\mathcal AA is order preserving on the quotient by additive constants. If a representative of the initial class is chosen so that f0≤A(f0)f_0\leq\mathcal A(f_0)f0​≤A(f0​), then representatives of the iterates fk+1=A(fk)f_{k+1}=\mathcal A(f_k)fk+1​=A(fk​) can be chosen to increase pointwise, remain uniformly bounded in oscillation, and converge to a fixed point. Since constants are free, any bounded initialization can be shifted downward to satisfy the subsolution inequality. The fixed point is the entropic potential, hence the associated Sinkhorn scalings converge.

Proof Sketch

The soft ccc-transform is order reversing. For example, if g≤g′g\leq g'g≤g′, then

−ϵlog⁡∫e(g−c)/ϵ  dβ≥−ϵlog⁡∫e(g′−c)/ϵ  dβ.-\epsilon\log\int e^{(g-c)/\epsilon}\,\d\beta \geq -\epsilon\log\int e^{(g'-c)/\epsilon}\,\d\beta .−ϵlog∫e(g−c)/ϵdβ≥−ϵlog∫e(g′−c)/ϵdβ.
(17)

The composition of two order-reversing transforms is order preserving. Soft transforms also commute with additive constants in projective form. Starting from a subsolution representative gives f0≤f1f_0\leq f_1f0​≤f1​, and order preservation gives representatives satisfying fk≤fk+1f_k\leq f_{k+1}fk​≤fk+1​. Soft-transform oscillation bounds, controlled by sup⁡c−inf⁡c\sup c-\inf csupc−infc, prevent escape to infinity after normalization. Monotone convergence, compactness of equicontinuous soft transforms, and continuity of A\mathcal AA give a fixed point. Uniqueness of entropic potentials up to constants identifies this fixed point with the Sinkhorn solution.

This proof is qualitative rather than quantitative, but it is conceptually useful: Sinkhorn is not only alternating projection or projective contraction; it is also a monotone fixed-point iteration on potential classes once constants are quotiented out.

Extension Beyond KL¶

The same monotone mechanism is not tied to the exponential law of KL regularization. For the ϕ\phiϕ-divergence regularized problems of Other Convex Regularizers, set

Φ:=ϕ∗,≥0.\Phi \eqdef \phi^{*,\geq0}.Φ:=ϕ∗,≥0.
(18)

The Fenchel dual ascent updates are generalized soft ccc-transforms. For fixed ggg, the update of fff is

gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)∈arg min⁡u∈R{ϵ∫YΦ ⁣(u+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dβ(y)−u}.g^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}(x) \in \uargmin{u\in\RR} \left\{ \epsilon \int_{\Yy} \Phi\!\left(\frac{u+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right) \d\beta(y) -u \right\}.gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)∈u∈Rargmin​{ϵ∫Y​Φ(ϵu+g(y)−c(x,y)​)dβ(y)−u}.
(19)

The update fc,ϵ,ϕf^{c,\epsilon,\phi}fc,ϵ,ϕ is obtained by exchanging (x,α,f)(x,\alpha,f)(x,α,f) and (y,β,g)(y,\beta,g)(y,β,g). In nonsmooth cases, the scalar optimality condition is the subgradient normalization

1∈∫Y∂Φ ⁣(gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dβ(y),1∈∫X∂Φ ⁣(f(x)+fc,ϵ,ϕ(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dα(x).1\in \int_{\Yy} \partial\Phi\!\left( \frac{g^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}(x)+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right) \d\beta(y), \qquad 1\in \int_{\Xx} \partial\Phi\!\left( \frac{f(x)+f^{c,\epsilon,\phi}(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right) \d\alpha(x).1∈∫Y​∂Φ(ϵgcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)​)dβ(y),1∈∫X​∂Φ(ϵf(x)+fc,ϵ,ϕ(y)−c(x,y)​)dα(x).
(20)

The integral of intervals is understood in the usual Aumann sense. When Φ\PhiΦ is differentiable and ρϕ=Φ′\rho_\phi=\Phi'ρϕ​=Φ′, this becomes

∫Yρϕ ⁣(gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dβ(y)=1,∫Xρϕ ⁣(f(x)+fc,ϵ,ϕ(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dα(x)=1.\int_{\Yy} \rho_\phi\!\left( \frac{g^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}(x)+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right) \d\beta(y) =1, \qquad \int_{\Xx} \rho_\phi\!\left( \frac{f(x)+f^{c,\epsilon,\phi}(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon} \right) \d\alpha(x) =1 .∫Y​ρϕ​(ϵgcˉ,ϵ,ϕ(x)+g(y)−c(x,y)​)dβ(y)=1,∫X​ρϕ​(ϵf(x)+fc,ϵ,ϕ(y)−c(x,y)​)dα(x)=1.
(21)

For KL, ρϕ(s)=es\rho_\phi(s)=e^sρϕ​(s)=es, so these are exactly Sinkhorn row and column normalizations. For Burg and quadratic penalties, the same relation holds with ρϕ(s)=(1−s)−1\rho_\phi(s)=(1-s)^{-1}ρϕ​(s)=(1−s)−1 or ρϕ(s)=(1+s)+\rho_\phi(s)=(1+s)_+ρϕ​(s)=(1+s)+​, respectively, on their domains.

Proposition: Monotonicity of Generalized Double Soft Transforms

Assume that the scalar minimizer sets defining gcˉ,ϵ,ϕg^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ and fc,ϵ,ϕf^{c,\epsilon,\phi}fc,ϵ,ϕ are nonempty compact intervals, and choose either the minimal or the maximal minimizer in every scalar problem. Then each selected generalized soft ccc-transform is order reversing and additively anti-homogeneous:

g≤g′⇒gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ≥g′cˉ,ϵ,ϕ,(g+λ)cˉ,ϵ,ϕ=gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ−λ.g\leq g' \Rightarrow g^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}\geq {g'}^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}, \qquad (g+\lambda)^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi} = g^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}-\lambda .g≤g′⇒gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ≥g′cˉ,ϵ,ϕ,(g+λ)cˉ,ϵ,ϕ=gcˉ,ϵ,ϕ−λ.
(22)

The same statements hold for f↦fc,ϵ,ϕf\mapsto f^{c,\epsilon,\phi}f↦fc,ϵ,ϕ. Consequently

Aϕ(f):=(fc,ϵ,ϕ)cˉ,ϵ,ϕ\mathcal A_\phi(f) \eqdef (f^{c,\epsilon,\phi})^{\bar c,\epsilon,\phi}Aϕ​(f):=(fc,ϵ,ϕ)cˉ,ϵ,ϕ
(23)

is order preserving and additively homogeneous:

f≤f′⇒Aϕ(f)≤Aϕ(f′),Aϕ(f+λ)=Aϕ(f)+λ.f\leq f' \Rightarrow \mathcal A_\phi(f)\leq\mathcal A_\phi(f'), \qquad \mathcal A_\phi(f+\lambda)=\mathcal A_\phi(f)+\lambda .f≤f′⇒Aϕ​(f)≤Aϕ​(f′),Aϕ​(f+λ)=Aϕ​(f)+λ.
(24)

In particular, this applies without mentioning selections whenever the scalar minimizers are unique.

Proof

Since

Φ(s)=sup⁡r≥0{sr−ϕ(r)},\Phi(s)=\sup_{r\geq0}\{sr-\phi(r)\},Φ(s)=r≥0sup​{sr−ϕ(r)},
(25)

Φ\PhiΦ is convex and nondecreasing. For fixed xxx, set

Hgx(u):=ϵ∫YΦ ⁣(u+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ) dβ(y)−u.H_g^x(u) \eqdef \epsilon \int_{\Yy} \Phi\!\left(\frac{u+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right) \d\beta(y) -u.Hgx​(u):=ϵ∫Y​Φ(ϵu+g(y)−c(x,y)​)dβ(y)−u.
(26)

If g≤g′g\leq g'g≤g′, write Δ(y)=g′(y)−g(y)≥0\Delta(y)=g'(y)-g(y)\geq0Δ(y)=g′(y)−g(y)≥0. The difference

Hg′x(u)−Hgx(u)=ϵ∫Y[Φ ⁣(u+g(y)+Δ(y)−c(x,y)ϵ)−Φ ⁣(u+g(y)−c(x,y)ϵ)] dβ(y)H_{g'}^x(u)-H_g^x(u) = \epsilon \int_{\Yy} \left[ \Phi\!\left(\frac{u+g(y)+\Delta(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{u+g(y)-c(x,y)}{\epsilon}\right) \right]\d\beta(y)Hg′x​(u)−Hgx​(u)=ϵ∫Y​[Φ(ϵu+g(y)+Δ(y)−c(x,y)​)−Φ(ϵu+g(y)−c(x,y)​)]dβ(y)
(27)

is nondecreasing in uuu, because for every δ≥0\delta\geq0δ≥0 the function s↦Φ(s+δ)−Φ(s)s\mapsto \Phi(s+\delta)-\Phi(s)s↦Φ(s+δ)−Φ(s) is nondecreasing by convexity of Φ\PhiΦ. Let I=arg min⁡HgxI=\argmin H_g^xI=argminHgx​ and I′=arg min⁡Hg′xI'=\argmin H_{g'}^xI′=argminHg′x​. If a=min⁡Ia=\min Ia=minI and a′=min⁡I′a'=\min I'a′=minI′ with a′>aa'>aa′>a, then the optimality inequalities

Hgx(a)≤Hgx(a′),Hg′x(a′)≤Hg′x(a)H_g^x(a)\leq H_g^x(a'), \qquad H_{g'}^x(a')\leq H_{g'}^x(a)Hgx​(a)≤Hgx​(a′),Hg′x​(a′)≤Hg′x​(a)
(28)

imply Hg′x(a′)−Hgx(a′)≤Hg′x(a)−Hgx(a)H_{g'}^x(a')-H_g^x(a')\leq H_{g'}^x(a)-H_g^x(a)Hg′x​(a′)−Hgx​(a′)≤Hg′x​(a)−Hgx​(a), contradicting monotonicity unless equality holds throughout. In the equality case, aaa is also a minimizer of Hg′xH_{g'}^xHg′x​, contradicting a′=min⁡I′a'=\min I'a′=minI′. Hence min⁡I′≤min⁡I\min I'\leq\min IminI′≤minI. The same argument with the maxima of the two minimizer intervals gives max⁡I′≤max⁡I\max I'\leq\max ImaxI′≤maxI. Thus both extremal selections are order reversing. Finally,

Hg+λx(u)=Hgx(u+λ)+λ,H_{g+\lambda}^x(u)=H_g^x(u+\lambda)+\lambda,Hg+λx​(u)=Hgx​(u+λ)+λ,
(29)

so the minimizer interval is shifted by −λ-\lambda−λ, which proves additive anti-homogeneity. The proof for f↦fc,ϵ,ϕf\mapsto f^{c,\epsilon,\phi}f↦fc,ϵ,ϕ is identical. Composing two order-reversing anti-homogeneous selections gives an order-preserving additively homogeneous double transform.

Thus the Fortet-type convergence mechanism is structural for dual block-coordinate ascent with convex density-ratio penalties. What is special to KL is the closed log-sum-exp formula and the associated positive-kernel projective contraction, not the underlying order preservation.

Definition: Variation Seminorm

For a bounded real-valued function hhh, the variation seminorm is

∥h∥V:=sup⁡h−inf⁡h.\norm{h}_V \eqdef \sup h-\inf h.∥h∥V​:=suph−infh.
(30)

It vanishes exactly on constant functions, hence becomes a norm after quotienting by additive constants.

This is the natural size for Sinkhorn potentials because adding constants changes their gauge but not the coupling.

Proposition: Topical Maps are Variation-Nonexpansive

Let EEE be a vector space of bounded functions, ordered pointwise, and write ∥⋅∥V\norm{\cdot}_V∥⋅∥V​ for the variation seminorm. Let T:E→E\mathcal T:E\to ET:E→E be monotone and additively homogeneous:

f≤g⇒T(f)≤T(g),T(f+λ)=T(f)+λ.f\leq g\Rightarrow \mathcal T(f)\leq\mathcal T(g), \qquad \mathcal T(f+\lambda)=\mathcal T(f)+\lambda.f≤g⇒T(f)≤T(g),T(f+λ)=T(f)+λ.
(31)

Then

∥T(f)−T(g)∥V≤∥f−g∥V.\norm{\mathcal T(f)-\mathcal T(g)}_V \leq \norm{f-g}_V .∥T(f)−T(g)∥V​≤∥f−g∥V​.
(32)

The same conclusion holds for order-reversing maps satisfying T(f+λ)=T(f)−λ\mathcal T(f+\lambda)=\mathcal T(f)-\lambdaT(f+λ)=T(f)−λ.

Proof

Set a=inf⁡(f−g)a=\inf(f-g)a=inf(f−g) and b=sup⁡(f−g)b=\sup(f-g)b=sup(f−g). Then

g+a≤f≤g+b.g+a\leq f\leq g+b.g+a≤f≤g+b.
(33)

If T\mathcal TT is order preserving and additively homogeneous, applying T\mathcal TT gives

T(g)+a≤T(f)≤T(g)+b.\mathcal T(g)+a \leq \mathcal T(f) \leq \mathcal T(g)+b.T(g)+a≤T(f)≤T(g)+b.
(34)

Hence every value of T(f)−T(g)\mathcal T(f)-\mathcal T(g)T(f)−T(g) lies in [a,b][a,b][a,b], so its oscillation is at most b−a=∥f−g∥Vb-a=\norm{f-g}_Vb−a=∥f−g∥V​. The order-reversing, anti-homogeneous case gives the same oscillation bound with the interval reversed.

Corollary: Soft Transforms are Nonexpansive

For every ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, the soft ccc-transforms are 1-Lipschitz for the variation seminorm. Consequently, the double Sinkhorn map, after quotienting constants, is also 1-Lipschitz for ∥⋅∥V\norm{\cdot}_V∥⋅∥V​.

Proof

The soft transform is order reversing and satisfies (g+λ)cˉ,ϵ=gcˉ,ϵ−λ(g+\lambda)^{\bar c,\epsilon}=g^{\bar c,\epsilon}-\lambda(g+λ)cˉ,ϵ=gcˉ,ϵ−λ, and similarly for the other block. The topical-map proposition applies to each block, and the composition of two 1-Lipschitz maps is 1-Lipschitz.

Remark: Topical maps and projective geometry

Order-preserving additively homogeneous maps are called topical maps in nonlinear Perron--Frobenius theory Lemmens & Nussbaum, 2012. Proposition Proposition: Topical Maps are Variation-Nonexpansive is the basic nonexpansiveness mechanism behind Fortet’s monotone argument.

Sinkhorn Convergence: Sublinear Robust Rate¶

Sinkhorn is cyclic coordinate ascent on the smooth dual objective; equivalently, it alternates KL projections on the two marginal constraint sets. The rate most useful for complexity estimates is the pre-asymptotic one: before a linear regime becomes visible, the dual objective gap decreases at order 1/k1/k1/k. Related robust Bregman-projection rates are developed in Peyré, 2026Altschuler et al., 2017Dvurechensky et al., 2018, and statistical consequences of entropic smoothing in Genevay et al., 2019Bigot et al., 2019.

Proposition: Pinsker Inequality

If p,q∈Δnp,q\in\simplex_np,q∈Δn​, then

∥p−q∥12≤2KL⁡(p∣q).\norm{p-q}_1^2 \leq 2\operatorname{KL}(p\mid q).∥p−q∥12​≤2KL(p∣q).
(35)
Proof

Let A={i:pi≥qi}A=\{i:p_i\geq q_i\}A={i:pi​≥qi​} and set a=∑i∈Apia=\sum_{i\in A}p_ia=∑i∈A​pi​, b=∑i∈Aqib=\sum_{i\in A}q_ib=∑i∈A​qi​. Then a−b=12∥p−q∥1a-b=\frac12\norm{p-q}_1a−b=21​∥p−q∥1​. Data processing for relative entropy gives

KL⁡(p∣q)≥alog⁡ab+(1−a)log⁡1−a1−b.\operatorname{KL}(p\mid q) \geq a\log\frac{a}{b} + (1-a)\log\frac{1-a}{1-b}.KL(p∣q)≥alogba​+(1−a)log1−b1−a​.
(36)

The binary relative entropy dominates 2(a−b)22(a-b)^22(a−b)2 because its second derivative, after subtracting 2(a−b)22(a-b)^22(a−b)2, is nonnegative. Therefore KL⁡(p∣q)≥12∥p−q∥12\operatorname{KL}(p\mid q)\geq\frac12\norm{p-q}_1^2KL(p∣q)≥21​∥p−q∥12​.

Proposition: A Compact O(1/k)O(1/k)O(1/k) Dual Rate

Assume that X\XxX and Y\YyY are compact, that ccc is bounded, and write R=sup⁡c−inf⁡cR=\sup c-\inf cR=supc−infc. Let (fk,gk)(f_k,g_k)(fk​,gk​) be Sinkhorn dual iterates normalized by ∫fk  dα=0\int f_k\,\d\alpha=0∫fk​dα=0, and let

Δk:=Dϵ(f⋆,g⋆)−Dϵ(fk,gk)\Delta_k \eqdef \mathcal D_\epsilon(f^\star,g^\star) - \mathcal D_\epsilon(f_k,g_k)Δk​:=Dϵ​(f⋆,g⋆)−Dϵ​(fk​,gk​)
(37)

be the dual suboptimality gap. Then there exists a numerical constant CCC such that

Δk≤CR2ϵ(k+1).\Delta_k \leq \frac{C R^2}{\epsilon(k+1)}.Δk​≤ϵ(k+1)CR2​.
(38)
Proof Sketch

The proof uses three ingredients. First, soft transforms bound the oscillation of normalized iterates and optima:

∥fk∥V+∥gk∥V+∥f⋆∥V+∥g⋆∥V≤CR.\norm{f_k}_V+\norm{g_k}_V + \norm{f^\star}_V+\norm{g^\star}_V \leq C R.∥fk​∥V​+∥gk​∥V​+∥f⋆∥V​+∥g⋆∥V​≤CR.
(39)

Second, each Sinkhorn half-step is an exact KL projection. The Pythagorean identity gives an ascent identity of the form

Dϵ(fk+1,gk+1)−Dϵ(fk,gk)=ϵ[KL⁡(π⋆∣πk)−KL⁡(π⋆∣πk+1)].\mathcal D_\epsilon(f_{k+1},g_{k+1}) - \mathcal D_\epsilon(f_k,g_k) = \epsilon\left[ \operatorname{KL}(\pi^\star\mid\pi_k) - \operatorname{KL}(\pi^\star\mid\pi_{k+1}) \right].Dϵ​(fk+1​,gk+1​)−Dϵ​(fk​,gk​)=ϵ[KL(π⋆∣πk​)−KL(π⋆∣πk+1​)].
(40)

Pinsker’s inequality converts this KL drop into control of marginal residuals. Third, convexity of the exponential dual objective yields

Δk2≤CR2ϵ(Dϵ(fk+1,gk+1)−Dϵ(fk,gk)).\Delta_k^2 \leq \frac{C R^2}{\epsilon} \left( \mathcal D_\epsilon(f_{k+1},g_{k+1}) - \mathcal D_\epsilon(f_k,g_k) \right).Δk2​≤ϵCR2​(Dϵ​(fk+1​,gk+1​)−Dϵ​(fk​,gk​)).
(41)

Summing the reciprocal inequality

1Δk+1−1Δk≥ϵCR2\frac1{\Delta_{k+1}}-\frac1{\Delta_k} \geq \frac{\epsilon}{C R^2}Δk+1​1​−Δk​1​≥CR2ϵ​
(42)

gives the displayed O(1/k)O(1/k)O(1/k) rate.

Corollary: Approximating Unregularized OT by Regularized Dual Costs

Consider histograms a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​ and a finite cost matrix CCC. Let Dϵ,k\mathcal D_{\epsilon,k}Dϵ,k​ be the KL-normalized entropic dual value after kkk Sinkhorn cycles, and let Δk\Delta_kΔk​ be its dual gap. Define the entropy-corrected lower bound

Lϵ,k:=Dϵ,k−ϵH(a)−ϵH(b),H(a)=−∑iailog⁡ai.L_{\epsilon,k} \eqdef \mathcal D_{\epsilon,k} - \epsilon H(a) - \epsilon H(b), \qquad H(a)=-\sum_i a_i\log a_i .Lϵ,k​:=Dϵ,k​−ϵH(a)−ϵH(b),H(a)=−i∑​ai​logai​.
(43)

Then

0≤OT⁡C(a,b)−Lϵ,k≤ϵlog⁡(nm)+Δk.0 \leq \operatorname{OT}_C(a,b)-L_{\epsilon,k} \leq \epsilon\log(nm)+\Delta_k .0≤OTC​(a,b)−Lϵ,k​≤ϵlog(nm)+Δk​.
(44)

Thus, choosing ϵ≤δ/(2log⁡(nm))\epsilon\leq\delta/(2\log(nm))ϵ≤δ/(2log(nm)) and running Sinkhorn until Δk≤δ/2\Delta_k\leq\delta/2Δk​≤δ/2 gives a δ\deltaδ-accurate lower bound on the unregularized OT value. Under the compact O(1/k)O(1/k)O(1/k) rate, it is sufficient to take

k+1≥4CR2log⁡(nm)δ2,k+1 \geq \frac{4 C R^2\log(nm)}{\delta^2},k+1≥δ24CR2log(nm)​,
(45)

up to constants and logarithmic stabilization factors.

The same identity yields computable stopping diagnostics. The KL drops are exactly marginal defects: after a row update the row marginal is correct and the remaining drop is measured by the column marginal, and conversely after a column update. Marginal violations therefore monitor both feasibility and the remaining dual gap, up to the bounded-radius constant.

Algorithm: Certified entropic approximation of discrete OT

Input: Cost matrix C∈Rn×mC\in\RR^{n\times m}C∈Rn×m, weights a,b\a,\ba,b, target accuracy δ>0\delta>0δ>0.

Output: Certified lower bound Lϵ,kL_{\epsilon,k}Lϵ,k​ for exact OT.

Set ϵ=δ2log⁡(nm).\epsilon=\frac{\delta}{2\log(nm)}.ϵ=2log(nm)δ​.

Initialize stabilized Sinkhorn potentials and set k=0k=0k=0, Δ^0=+∞\widehat\Delta_0=+\inftyΔ0​=+∞.

While Δ^k>δ/2\widehat\Delta_k>\delta/2Δk​>δ/2 do:

Set k←k+1k\leftarrow k+1k←k+1.

Compute one row soft-transform update and one column soft-transform update in stabilized log-domain variables.

Compute the entropic dual value Dϵ,k\mathcal D_{\epsilon,k}Dϵ,k​ and the certified gap upper bound Δ^k\widehat\Delta_kΔk​.

Return Lϵ,k=Dϵ,k−ϵH(a)−ϵH(b),0≤LC(a,b)−Lϵ,k≤δ.L_{\epsilon,k} = \mathcal D_{\epsilon,k} - \epsilon\HD(\a)-\epsilon\HD(\b), \qquad 0\leq\MKD_{\C}(\a,\b)-L_{\epsilon,k}\leq\delta.Lϵ,k​=Dϵ,k​−ϵH(a)−ϵH(b),0≤LC​(a,b)−Lϵ,k​≤δ.

Sinkhorn Convergence: Linear Hilbert Metric Rate¶

Hilbert’s projective metric gives a complementary convergence mechanism. Instead of following objective values, it measures distances between positive scaling vectors modulo global multiplication. Positive kernels are contractions in this geometry, yielding a global linear convergence statement Franklin & Lorenz, 1989.

Definition: Hilbert Metric

On R+,∗n\RR_{+,*}^nR+,∗n​, Hilbert’s projective metric is

H(u,u′):=∥log⁡u−log⁡u′∥V,\mathsf H(u,u') \eqdef \norm{\log u-\log u'}_V,H(u,u′):=∥logu−logu′∥V​,
(46)

where, for vectors, ∥z∥V=max⁡izi−min⁡izi\norm{z}_V=\max_i z_i-\min_i z_i∥z∥V​=maxi​zi​−mini​zi​.

Multiplying both vectors by arbitrary positive constants does not change this quantity, so it is a distance only after passing to projective classes.

Proposition: Hilbert Metric on the Projective Cone

The function H\mathsf HH defines a complete distance on the projective cone R+,∗n/∼\RR_{+,*}^n/\simR+,∗n​/∼, where u∼u′u\sim u'u∼u′ means u=su′u=su'u=su′ for some s>0s>0s>0.

Proof

The map u↦log⁡uu\mapsto\log uu↦logu identifies R+,∗n/∼\RR_{+,*}^n/\simR+,∗n​/∼ with the quotient vector space Rn/span⁡(1n)\RR^n/\operatorname{span}(\mathbf 1_n)Rn/span(1n​). The variation seminorm vanishes exactly on constant vectors, so it induces a norm on this quotient. Symmetry, the triangle inequality, separation and completeness follow from the corresponding finite-dimensional norm properties.

Hilbert’s metric was introduced by Birkhoff and Samelson to give quantitative proofs of the Perron--Frobenius theorem Birkhoff, 1957Samelson, 1957. Sinkhorn can be viewed as a nonlinear matrix-scaling analogue of this theory.

Theorem: Birkhoff Contraction Theorem

Let K∈R+,∗n×mK\in\RR_{+,*}^{n\times m}K∈R+,∗n×m​. For v,v′∈R+,∗mv,v'\in\RR_{+,*}^mv,v′∈R+,∗m​,

H(Kv,Kv′)≤λ(K)H(v,v′),\mathsf H(Kv,Kv') \leq \lambda(K)\mathsf H(v,v'),H(Kv,Kv′)≤λ(K)H(v,v′),
(47)

where

λ(K):=η(K)−1η(K)+1<1,η(K):=max⁡i,j,k,ℓKi,kKj,ℓKj,kKi,ℓ.\lambda(K) \eqdef \frac{\sqrt{\eta(K)}-1}{\sqrt{\eta(K)}+1} <1, \qquad \eta(K) \eqdef \max_{i,j,k,\ell} \frac{K_{i,k}K_{j,\ell}}{K_{j,k}K_{i,\ell}}.λ(K):=η(K)​+1η(K)​−1​<1,η(K):=i,j,k,ℓmax​Kj,k​Ki,ℓ​Ki,k​Kj,ℓ​​.
(48)
Proof Sketch

For a positive linear map AAA on a cone, define its projective diameter

Δ(A):=sup⁡u,v>0H(Au,Av).\Delta(A) \eqdef \sup_{u,v>0}\mathsf H(Au,Av).Δ(A):=u,v>0sup​H(Au,Av).
(49)

The Birkhoff--Hopf estimate states

H(Au,Av)≤tanh⁡(Δ(A)/4)H(u,v).\mathsf H(Au,Av) \leq \tanh(\Delta(A)/4)\mathsf H(u,v).H(Au,Av)≤tanh(Δ(A)/4)H(u,v).
(50)

For a positive matrix KKK, the projective diameter is

Δ(K)=log⁡η(K).\Delta(K)=\log\eta(K).Δ(K)=logη(K).
(51)

Substituting into tanh⁡(Δ/4)\tanh(\Delta/4)tanh(Δ/4) gives λ(K)=(η(K)−1)/(η(K)+1)\lambda(K)=(\sqrt{\eta(K)}-1)/(\sqrt{\eta(K)}+1)λ(K)=(η(K)​−1)/(η(K)​+1).

Theorem: Linear Convergence of Sinkhorn

Let (u(ℓ),v(ℓ))(u^{(\ell)},v^{(\ell)})(u(ℓ),v(ℓ)) be Sinkhorn scaling iterates and (u⋆,v⋆)(u^\star,v^\star)(u⋆,v⋆) optimal scalings. Then

H(u(ℓ),u⋆)=O(λ(K)2ℓ),H(v(ℓ),v⋆)=O(λ(K)2ℓ).\mathsf H(u^{(\ell)},u^\star)=O(\lambda(K)^{2\ell}), \qquad \mathsf H(v^{(\ell)},v^\star)=O(\lambda(K)^{2\ell}).H(u(ℓ),u⋆)=O(λ(K)2ℓ),H(v(ℓ),v⋆)=O(λ(K)2ℓ).
(52)

Moreover, for P(ℓ)=diag⁡(u(ℓ))Kdiag⁡(v(ℓ))P^{(\ell)}=\operatorname{diag}(u^{(\ell)})K\operatorname{diag}(v^{(\ell)})P(ℓ)=diag(u(ℓ))Kdiag(v(ℓ)),

H(u(ℓ),u⋆)≤H(P(ℓ)1m,a)1−λ(K),H(v(ℓ),v⋆)≤H((P(ℓ))⊤1n,b)1−λ(K).\mathsf H(u^{(\ell)},u^\star) \leq \frac{\mathsf H(P^{(\ell)}\mathbf 1_m,a)}{1-\lambda(K)}, \qquad \mathsf H(v^{(\ell)},v^\star) \leq \frac{\mathsf H((P^{(\ell)})^\top\mathbf 1_n,b)}{1-\lambda(K)}.H(u(ℓ),u⋆)≤1−λ(K)H(P(ℓ)1m​,a)​,H(v(ℓ),v⋆)≤1−λ(K)H((P(ℓ))⊤1n​,b)​.
(53)

The primal logarithmic error satisfies

∥log⁡P(ℓ)−log⁡P⋆∥∞≤H(u(ℓ),u⋆)+H(v(ℓ),v⋆).\norm{\log P^{(\ell)}-\log P^\star}_\infty \leq \mathsf H(u^{(\ell)},u^\star)+\mathsf H(v^{(\ell)},v^\star).∥∥​logP(ℓ)−logP⋆∥∥​∞​≤H(u(ℓ),u⋆)+H(v(ℓ),v⋆).
(54)
Proof Sketch

Using the invariance of Hilbert’s metric under coordinatewise inversion and positive multiplication,

H ⁣(aKv(ℓ),aKv⋆)=H(Kv(ℓ),Kv⋆)≤λ(K)H(v(ℓ),v⋆).\mathsf H\!\left( \frac{a}{Kv^{(\ell)}}, \frac{a}{Kv^\star} \right) = \mathsf H(Kv^{(\ell)},Kv^\star) \leq \lambda(K)\mathsf H(v^{(\ell)},v^\star).H(Kv(ℓ)a​,Kv⋆a​)=H(Kv(ℓ),Kv⋆)≤λ(K)H(v(ℓ),v⋆).
(55)

The same estimate holds for the other block. Applying it twice gives the linear rate. The a posteriori marginal-residual bounds follow from the triangle inequality and the fixed-point equations. The logarithmic primal bound follows because log⁡P(ℓ)−log⁡P⋆\log P^{(\ell)}-\log P^\starlogP(ℓ)−logP⋆ is the sum of the two logarithmic scaling errors.

For bounded cost, the contraction can also be read directly on the dual potentials. With Kϵ=e−c/ϵK_\epsilon=e^{-c/\epsilon}Kϵ​=e−c/ϵ and R=sup⁡c−inf⁡cR=\sup c-\inf cR=supc−infc,

λ(Kϵ)≤tanh⁡(R/(2ϵ)).\lambda(K_\epsilon) \leq \tanh(R/(2\epsilon)).λ(Kϵ​)≤tanh(R/(2ϵ)).
(56)

This is a useful global statement, but it also explains a limitation: the worst-case factor becomes exponentially close to one as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0. In practice, one often observes a much better local linear regime after enough iterations. The same Hilbert-metric mechanism extends beyond finite matrices to positive integral operators under compactness and positivity assumptions, but Gaussian distributions with quadratic cost require a different approach.

The interactive demo below separates the observed marginal residual from two theoretical guides. The O(1/k)O(1/k)O(1/k) curve captures the robust pre-asymptotic message, while the Hilbert curve shows how pessimistic the global contraction bound becomes when the Gibbs kernel is close to degenerate.

Interactive panel. This exploratory panel plots convergence guides for Sinkhorn iterations. Use epsilon and condition controls to compare observed residuals with pessimistic global contraction bounds.

Entropic Optimal Transport Between Gaussians¶

Gaussian marginals provide an explicit finite-dimensional model of Sinkhorn’s behavior. The soft ccc-transform preserves quadratic potentials, the optimal entropic coupling is Gaussian, and the value can be written with matrix square roots Janati et al., 2020. This is the entropic counterpart of the Gaussian W2\Wass_2W2​ and Bures formula.

Proposition: Quadratic Closure of Sinkhorn Iterates

Let β=N(mβ,Σβ)\beta=\mathcal N(m_\beta,\Sigma_\beta)β=N(mβ​,Σβ​) on Rd\RR^dRd and take c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2. If g(y)g(y)g(y) is a quadratic polynomial such that the Gaussian integral below is finite, then the soft transform

f(x)=−ϵlog⁡∫exp⁡ ⁣(g(y)−∥x−y∥2ϵ)  dβ(y)f(x) = -\epsilon\log \int \exp\!\left(\frac{g(y)-\norm{x-y}^2}{\epsilon}\right) \,\d\beta(y)f(x)=−ϵlog∫exp(ϵg(y)−∥x−y∥2​)dβ(y)
(57)

is a quadratic polynomial in xxx. In particular, starting Sinkhorn from g0=0g_0=0g0​=0 gives

f1(x)=ϵ2log⁡det⁡ ⁣(I+2Σβϵ)+ϵ⟨x−mβ,(ϵI+2Σβ)−1(x−mβ)⟩.f_1(x) = \frac{\epsilon}{2} \log\det\!\left(I+\frac{2\Sigma_\beta}{\epsilon}\right) + \epsilon \left\langle x-m_\beta, (\epsilon I+2\Sigma_\beta)^{-1}(x-m_\beta) \right\rangle .f1​(x)=2ϵ​logdet(I+ϵ2Σβ​​)+ϵ⟨x−mβ​,(ϵI+2Σβ​)−1(x−mβ​)⟩.
(58)
Proof

The exponent is the sum of a quadratic polynomial in yyy and the logarithm of the Gaussian density of β\betaβ. Completing the square in yyy evaluates the integral as a positive constant times the exponential of a quadratic polynomial in xxx. Taking −ϵlog⁡-\epsilon\log−ϵlog therefore gives a quadratic polynomial.

Proposition: Balanced Entropic OT Between Gaussians

Let α=N(mα,Σα)\alpha=\mathcal N(m_\alpha,\Sigma_\alpha)α=N(mα​,Σα​) and β=N(mβ,Σβ)\beta=\mathcal N(m_\beta,\Sigma_\beta)β=N(mβ​,Σβ​) with positive-definite covariances, and let

Σα1/2Σβ1/2=Udiag⁡(σi)V⊤\Sigma_\alpha^{1/2}\Sigma_\beta^{1/2} = U\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_i)V^\topΣα1/2​Σβ1/2​=Udiag(σi​)V⊤
(59)

be a singular-value decomposition. For the balanced objective

min⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫∥x−y∥2  dπ(x,y)+ϵKL⁡(π∣α⊗β),\min_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \int\norm{x-y}^2\,\d\pi(x,y) + \epsilon\operatorname{KL}(\pi\mid\alpha\otimes\beta),π∈Π(α,β)min​∫∥x−y∥2dπ(x,y)+ϵKL(π∣α⊗β),
(60)

the optimizer is Gaussian with cross-covariance

Kϵ=Σα1/2Udiag⁡(si)V⊤Σβ1/2,si=ϵ2+16σi2−ϵ4σi.K_\epsilon = \Sigma_\alpha^{1/2} U\operatorname{diag}(s_i)V^\top \Sigma_\beta^{1/2}, \qquad s_i = \frac{\sqrt{\epsilon^2+16\sigma_i^2}-\epsilon}{4\sigma_i}.Kϵ​=Σα1/2​Udiag(si​)V⊤Σβ1/2​,si​=4σi​ϵ2+16σi2​​−ϵ​.
(61)

The optimal value is

∥mα−mβ∥2+tr⁡(Σα)+tr⁡(Σβ)+∑i(−2σisi−ϵ2log⁡(1−si2)).\norm{m_\alpha-m_\beta}^2 + \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma_\alpha) + \operatorname{tr}(\Sigma_\beta) + \sum_i \left( -2\sigma_i s_i - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\log(1-s_i^2) \right).∥mα​−mβ​∥2+tr(Σα​)+tr(Σβ​)+i∑​(−2σi​si​−2ϵ​log(1−si2​)).
(62)

As ϵ↓0\epsilon\downarrow0ϵ↓0, si→1s_i\to1si​→1 and the full covariance contribution converges to the Bures--Wasserstein covariance term.

Proof Sketch

For any coupling, replace (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) by the Gaussian vector with the same mean and covariance. The quadratic cost is unchanged, and Gaussian laws maximize entropy at fixed covariance, so the relative entropy cannot increase. It is therefore enough to optimize over Gaussian couplings.

Writing the cross-covariance as K=Σα1/2SΣβ1/2K=\Sigma_\alpha^{1/2}S\Sigma_\beta^{1/2}K=Σα1/2​SΣβ1/2​, the block covariance constraint is equivalent to the singular values of SSS being at most one. The cost depends on KKK through −2tr⁡(K)-2\operatorname{tr}(K)−2tr(K), while

KL⁡(π∣α⊗β)=−12log⁡det⁡(I−SS⊤).\operatorname{KL}(\pi\mid\alpha\otimes\beta) = -\frac12\log\det(I-SS^\top).KL(π∣α⊗β)=−21​logdet(I−SS⊤).
(63)

Von Neumann’s trace inequality aligns SSS with the singular vectors of Σα1/2Σβ1/2\Sigma_\alpha^{1/2}\Sigma_\beta^{1/2}Σα1/2​Σβ1/2​, so the problem separates into

min⁡0≤s<1−2σis−ϵ2log⁡(1−s2).\min_{0\leq s<1} -2\sigma_i s - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\log(1-s^2).0≤s<1min​−2σi​s−2ϵ​log(1−s2).
(64)

The first-order condition is 2σi=ϵs/(1−s2)2\sigma_i=\epsilon s/(1-s^2)2σi​=ϵs/(1−s2), whose positive solution is the displayed sis_isi​.

Corollary: Gaussian Sinkhorn Divergence and Smoothed Bures Term

For r>0r>0r>0, define

τϵ(r):=ϵ2+16r2−ϵ4r,ψϵ(r):=−2r τϵ(r)−ϵ2log⁡(1−τϵ(r)2).\tau_\epsilon(r) \eqdef \frac{\sqrt{\epsilon^2+16r^2}-\epsilon}{4r}, \qquad \psi_\epsilon(r) \eqdef -2r\,\tau_\epsilon(r) - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\log(1-\tau_\epsilon(r)^2).τϵ​(r):=4rϵ2+16r2​−ϵ​,ψϵ​(r):=−2rτϵ​(r)−2ϵ​log(1−τϵ​(r)2).
(65)

If σi(Σ,Λ)\sigma_i(\Sigma,\Lambda)σi​(Σ,Λ) denotes the singular values of Σ1/2Λ1/2\Sigma^{1/2}\Lambda^{1/2}Σ1/2Λ1/2 and λi(Σ)\lambda_i(\Sigma)λi​(Σ) the eigenvalues of Σ\SigmaΣ, then the debiased Gaussian Sinkhorn divergence is

L‾∥⋅−⋅∥2ϵ(α,β)=∥mα−mβ∥2+Bϵ(Σα,Σβ)2,\overline{\mathcal L}_{\norm{\cdot-\cdot}^2}^{\epsilon}(\alpha,\beta) = \norm{m_\alpha-m_\beta}^2 + \mathcal B_\epsilon(\Sigma_\alpha,\Sigma_\beta)^2,L∥⋅−⋅∥2ϵ​(α,β)=∥mα​−mβ​∥2+Bϵ​(Σα​,Σβ​)2,
(66)

where

Bϵ(Σ,Λ)2:=∑iψϵ(σi(Σ,Λ))−12∑iψϵ(λi(Σ))−12∑iψϵ(λi(Λ)).\mathcal B_\epsilon(\Sigma,\Lambda)^2 \eqdef \sum_i\psi_\epsilon(\sigma_i(\Sigma,\Lambda)) - \frac12\sum_i\psi_\epsilon(\lambda_i(\Sigma)) - \frac12\sum_i\psi_\epsilon(\lambda_i(\Lambda)).Bϵ​(Σ,Λ)2:=i∑​ψϵ​(σi​(Σ,Λ))−21​i∑​ψϵ​(λi​(Σ))−21​i∑​ψϵ​(λi​(Λ)).
(67)

Moreover Bϵ(Σ,Λ)2→B(Σ,Λ)2\mathcal B_\epsilon(\Sigma,\Lambda)^2\to \mathcal B(\Sigma,\Lambda)^2Bϵ​(Σ,Λ)2→B(Σ,Λ)2 as ϵ↓0\epsilon\downarrow0ϵ↓0.

Proof Sketch

The raw Gaussian entropic value is the squared mean displacement plus two trace terms and the spectral sum ∑iψϵ(σi(Σα,Σβ))\sum_i\psi_\epsilon(\sigma_i(\Sigma_\alpha,\Sigma_\beta))∑i​ψϵ​(σi​(Σα​,Σβ​)). Applying the same formula to the self-costs (α,α)(\alpha,\alpha)(α,α) and (β,β)(\beta,\beta)(β,β) replaces the cross singular values by the eigenvalues of Σα\Sigma_\alphaΣα​ and Σβ\Sigma_\betaΣβ​.

In the debiased polarization formula, the trace terms cancel:

tr⁡Σα+tr⁡Σβ−12(2tr⁡Σα)−12(2tr⁡Σβ)=0.\operatorname{tr}\Sigma_\alpha+\operatorname{tr}\Sigma_\beta - \frac12(2\operatorname{tr}\Sigma_\alpha) - \frac12(2\operatorname{tr}\Sigma_\beta) =0.trΣα​+trΣβ​−21​(2trΣα​)−21​(2trΣβ​)=0.
(68)

The polarization of the squared mean terms leaves ∥mα−mβ∥2\norm{m_\alpha-m_\beta}^2∥mα​−mβ​∥2. Finally, τϵ(r)→1\tau_\epsilon(r)\to1τϵ​(r)→1 and ϵlog⁡(1−τϵ(r)2)→0\epsilon\log(1-\tau_\epsilon(r)^2)\to0ϵlog(1−τϵ​(r)2)→0, so ψϵ(r)→−2r\psi_\epsilon(r)\to-2rψϵ​(r)→−2r. The covariance limit is therefore

tr⁡Σ+tr⁡Λ−2∑iσi(Σ,Λ),\operatorname{tr}\Sigma+\operatorname{tr}\Lambda - 2\sum_i\sigma_i(\Sigma,\Lambda),trΣ+trΛ−2i∑​σi​(Σ,Λ),
(69)

which is the Bures--Wasserstein covariance formula.

The controls below expose exactly the quantities in the formula: ϵ\epsilonϵ sets the singular-value shrinkage, anisotropy changes the eigenvalues, and the angle changes the covariance misalignment.

Interactive panel. This exploratory panel exposes the Gaussian formula directly. Use epsilon, anisotropy, and angle to see how entropic shrinkage changes the covariance term.

Proposition: One-Dimensional Gaussian Sinkhorn Rate

Consider α=β=N(0,1)\alpha=\beta=\mathcal N(0,1)α=β=N(0,1) on R\RRR with c(x,y)=(x−y)2c(x,y)=(x-y)^2c(x,y)=(x−y)2. If a dual potential has the form gq(y)=qy2+cstg_q(y)=q y^2+\text{cst}gq​(y)=qy2+cst, then one soft transform has quadratic coefficient

Tϵ(q)=1−11−q+ϵ/2,q<1+ϵ/2.T_\epsilon(q) = 1-\frac{1}{1-q+\epsilon/2}, \qquad q<1+\epsilon/2.Tϵ​(q)=1−1−q+ϵ/21​,q<1+ϵ/2.
(70)

One full Sinkhorn cycle acts as q↦Tϵ(Tϵ(q))q\mapsto T_\epsilon(T_\epsilon(q))q↦Tϵ​(Tϵ​(q)). The fixed point q⋆=Tϵ(q⋆)q_\star=T_\epsilon(q_\star)q⋆​=Tϵ​(q⋆​) is determined by

A⋆2−ϵ2A⋆−1=0,A⋆:=1−q⋆+ϵ2=ϵ+ϵ2+164.A_\star^2-\frac{\epsilon}{2}A_\star-1=0, \qquad A_\star \eqdef 1-q_\star+\frac{\epsilon}{2} = \frac{\epsilon+\sqrt{\epsilon^2+16}}{4}.A⋆2​−2ϵ​A⋆​−1=0,A⋆​:=1−q⋆​+2ϵ​=4ϵ+ϵ2+16​​.
(71)

Consequently the local asymptotic contraction factor of one full Sinkhorn cycle on the quadratic coefficient is

ρϵ=A⋆−4=(4ϵ+ϵ2+16)4.\rho_\epsilon = A_\star^{-4} = \left(\frac{4}{\epsilon+\sqrt{\epsilon^2+16}}\right)^4 .ρϵ​=A⋆−4​=(ϵ+ϵ2+16​4​)4.
(72)
Proof

Completing the square in

∫exp⁡ ⁣(qy2−(x−y)2ϵ)  dN(0,1)(y)\int \exp\!\left( \frac{q y^2-(x-y)^2}{\epsilon} \right) \,\d\mathcal N(0,1)(y)∫exp(ϵqy2−(x−y)2​)dN(0,1)(y)
(73)

gives the coefficient Tϵ(q)T_\epsilon(q)Tϵ​(q). The fixed-point equation q⋆=1−1/A⋆q_\star=1-1/A_\starq⋆​=1−1/A⋆​, together with q⋆=1+ϵ/2−A⋆q_\star=1+\epsilon/2-A_\starq⋆​=1+ϵ/2−A⋆​, gives

A⋆2−ϵ2A⋆−1=0.A_\star^2-\frac{\epsilon}{2}A_\star-1=0.A⋆2​−2ϵ​A⋆​−1=0.
(74)

The positive solution is the displayed A⋆A_\starA⋆​. Since

Tϵ′(q)=−1(1−q+ϵ/2)2,T_\epsilon'(q) = -\frac{1}{(1-q+\epsilon/2)^2},Tϵ′​(q)=−(1−q+ϵ/2)21​,
(75)

the derivative of the full-cycle map at the fixed point is Tϵ′(q⋆)2=A⋆−4T_\epsilon'(q_\star)^2=A_\star^{-4}Tϵ′​(q⋆​)2=A⋆−4​.

This scalar calculation illustrates the general Gaussian convergence picture of Chizat, Delalande and Vaskevicius Chizat et al., 2024: the rate improves when ϵ\epsilonϵ is large or the covariance scales overlap well, and deteriorates in the small-temperature limit where the entropic coupling approaches a deterministic Brenier map.

Algorithm: Closed-form Gaussian Sinkhorn coupling

Input: Gaussian marginals α=N(mα,Σα)\al=\Gaussian(\mean_\al,\cov_\al)α=N(mα​,Σα​), β=N(mβ,Σβ)\be=\Gaussian(\mean_\be,\cov_\be)β=N(mβ​,Σβ​), scale ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0.

Output: Gaussian entropic coupling covariance.

Compute singular value decomposition Σα1/2Σβ1/2=Udiag⁡(σi)V⊤.\cov_\al^{1/2}\cov_\be^{1/2} = U\diag(\sigma_i)V^\top .Σα1/2​Σβ1/2​=Udiag(σi​)V⊤.

For each σi>0\sigma_i>0σi​>0 do

si=ϵ2+16σi2−ϵ4σi.s_i=\frac{\sqrt{\epsilon^2+16\sigma_i^2}-\epsilon}{4\sigma_i}.si​=4σi​ϵ2+16σi2​​−ϵ​.

Set cross-covariance: Kϵ=Σα1/2Udiag⁡(si)V⊤Σβ1/2.K_\epsilon = \cov_\al^{1/2}U\diag(s_i)V^\top\cov_\be^{1/2}.Kϵ​=Σα1/2​Udiag(si​)V⊤Σβ1/2​. Return Gaussian coupling with means (mα,mβ)(\mean_\al,\mean_\be)(mα​,mβ​), marginal covariances (Σα,Σβ)(\cov_\al,\cov_\be)(Σα​,Σβ​), and cross-covariance KϵK_\epsilonKϵ​.

Continuous ε\varepsilonε-Sinkhorn Flow¶

The preceding arguments view Sinkhorn as a discrete fixed-point iteration. A complementary picture, developed by Berman Berman, 2017, lets the potentials evolve in continuous time. The limit is not the fixed-temperature iteration itself: one lets the grid resolution kkk grow while setting the entropic temperature to ϵ=k−1\epsilon=k^{-1}ϵ=k−1, so that the inverse temperature 1/ϵ1/\epsilon1/ϵ grows at the same rate; the Sinkhorn iterates are then observed at continuous time t=m/kt=m/kt=m/k. In this high-resolution, vanishing-temperature scaling, Sinkhorn becomes a flow of potentials whose stationary equation is the Monge--Ampere equation for the optimal map. This viewpoint connects matrix scaling with the geometric theory of real and complex Monge--Ampere flows.

Definition: Continuous ε\varepsilonε-Sinkhorn Flow

Let α=e−F dx\alpha=e^{-F}\,dxα=e−Fdx and β=e−G dx\beta=e^{-G}\,dxβ=e−Gdx be smooth positive probability densities on the unit-volume flat torus Td\mathbb T^dTd. A gauge-fixed potential ut:Td→Ru_t:\mathbb T^d\to\mathbb Rut​:Td→R, with ∫Tdut dx=0\int_{\mathbb T^d}u_t\,dx=0∫Td​ut​dx=0, follows the continuous ε\varepsilonε-Sinkhorn flow if

∂tut(x)=log⁡det⁡ ⁣(I+∇2ut(x))−G(x+∇ut(x))+F(x)−rˉt,\partial_t u_t(x) = \log\det\!\big(I+\nabla^2u_t(x)\big) -G\big(x+\nabla u_t(x)\big)+F(x)-\bar r_t,∂t​ut​(x)=logdet(I+∇2ut​(x))−G(x+∇ut​(x))+F(x)−rˉt​,
(76)

where I+∇2ut≻0I+\nabla^2u_t\succ0I+∇2ut​≻0 and rˉt\bar r_trˉt​ is the spatial mean of the bracketed right-hand side. This subtraction fixes the additive gauge of the potential.

With the quadratic cost convention, utu_tut​ represents one member of the Sinkhorn potential pair (ut,vt)(u_t,v_t)(ut​,vt​), while the companion target potential vtv_tvt​ is recovered, at the discrete level, by the corresponding soft ccc-transform. Berman’s scaled log-Sinkhorn iteration can be written schematically as

um+1(k)−um(k)=k−1log⁡ρkum(k),u_{m+1}^{(k)}-u_m^{(k)} = k^{-1}\log \rho_{k u_m^{(k)}} ,um+1(k)​−um(k)​=k−1logρkum(k)​​,
(77)

and a Laplace expansion gives

ρku(x)=det⁡(I+∇2u(x))eF(x)−G(x+∇u(x))(1+O(k−1)).\rho_{ku}(x) = \det(I+\nabla^2u(x)) e^{F(x)-G(x+\nabla u(x))} \big(1+O(k^{-1})\big).ρku​(x)=det(I+∇2u(x))eF(x)−G(x+∇u(x))(1+O(k−1)).
(78)

Under the smoothness and strict quasi-convexity assumptions of Berman’s convergence theorem, um(k)→utu_m^{(k)}\to u_tum(k)​→ut​ with t=m/kt=m/kt=m/k, and the above asymptotic leads to the continuous flow above, after the gauge normalization. The word “epsilon” refers to the vanishing entropic temperature before this rescaling; no explicit ϵ\epsilonϵ remains in the normalized limiting PDE. The equation is a real parabolic Monge--Ampere equation. Its Kahler analogue is the parabolic complex Monge--Ampere equation, which explains the link with geometric Monge--Ampere theory.

Proposition: Stationary Continuous Sinkhorn Potentials

Assume that a smooth stationary solution satisfies I+∇2u≻0I+\nabla^2u\succ0I+∇2u≻0, and define T(x)=x+∇u(x)T(x)=x+\nabla u(x)T(x)=x+∇u(x). Then T#α=βT_{\#}\alpha=\betaT#​α=β. Conversely, any smooth map of the form T=I+∇uT=I+\nabla uT=I+∇u with I+∇2u≻0I+\nabla^2u\succ0I+∇2u≻0 and T#α=βT_{\#}\alpha=\betaT#​α=β solves the stationary equation, up to the additive gauge of uuu.

Proof

At stationarity,

log⁡det⁡ ⁣(I+∇2u(x))−G(T(x))+F(x)=c.\log\det\!\big(I+\nabla^2u(x)\big)-G(T(x))+F(x)=c .logdet(I+∇2u(x))−G(T(x))+F(x)=c.
(79)

Exponentiating, and using ∇T=I+∇2u\nabla T=I+\nabla^2u∇T=I+∇2u, gives

e−G(T(x))det⁡(∇T(x))=e−F(x)ec.e^{-G(T(x))}\det(\nabla T(x)) = e^{-F(x)}e^c .e−G(T(x))det(∇T(x))=e−F(x)ec.
(80)

Because TTT is homotopic to the identity and I+∇2u≻0I+\nabla^2u\succ0I+∇2u≻0, it is an orientation-preserving local diffeomorphism of degree one; hence it is a diffeomorphism of the torus. Since both measures have mass one, integration and change of variables give ec=1e^c=1ec=1. The identity is then exactly the Jacobian equation for T#(e−Fdx)=e−GdxT_{\#}(e^{-F}dx)=e^{-G}dxT#​(e−Fdx)=e−Gdx. The converse follows by reversing the argument.

Gaussian Closure¶

Gaussian marginals give a finite-dimensional test case for the continuous flow. The theorem above is stated on the flat torus, but the same local Laplace calculation can be read formally on Rd\RR^dRd for confining Gaussian densities. Write α=N(mα,Σα)\alpha=\Gaussian(m_\alpha,\Sigma_\alpha)α=N(mα​,Σα​) and β=N(mβ,Σβ)\beta=\Gaussian(m_\beta,\Sigma_\beta)β=N(mβ​,Σβ​), and restrict the potential to the quadratic ansatz for which

Tt(x):=x+∇ut(x)=qt+Bt(x−mα),Bt∈S++d.T_t(x)\eqdef x+\nabla u_t(x) = q_t+B_t(x-m_\alpha), \qquad B_t\in\mathbb S_{++}^d .Tt​(x):=x+∇ut​(x)=qt​+Bt​(x−mα​),Bt​∈S++d​.
(81)

Taking the spatial gradient of the continuous ε\varepsilonε-Sinkhorn PDE removes the additive gauge. Since

F(x)=12⟨x−mα,Σα−1(x−mα)⟩+cst,G(y)=12⟨y−mβ,Σβ−1(y−mβ)⟩+cst,F(x)=\frac12\langle x-m_\alpha,\Sigma_\alpha^{-1}(x-m_\alpha)\rangle+\mathrm{cst}, \qquad G(y)=\frac12\langle y-m_\beta,\Sigma_\beta^{-1}(y-m_\beta)\rangle+\mathrm{cst},F(x)=21​⟨x−mα​,Σα−1​(x−mα​)⟩+cst,G(y)=21​⟨y−mβ​,Σβ−1​(y−mβ​)⟩+cst,
(82)

coefficient matching in the identity ∂tTt=∇∂tut\partial_tT_t=\nabla\partial_tu_t∂t​Tt​=∇∂t​ut​ gives

B˙t=Σα−1−BtΣβ−1Bt,q˙t=−BtΣβ−1(qt−mβ).\dot B_t=\Sigma_\alpha^{-1}-B_t\Sigma_\beta^{-1}B_t, \qquad \dot q_t=-B_t\Sigma_\beta^{-1}(q_t-m_\beta).B˙t​=Σα−1​−Bt​Σβ−1​Bt​,q˙​t​=−Bt​Σβ−1​(qt​−mβ​).
(83)

Thus the parabolic Monge--Ampere equation reduces, on the Gaussian ansatz, to a Riccati evolution for the linear part of the transport. The image mean is qtq_tqt​, and the image covariance is

Σt=BtΣαBt,Σ˙t=B˙tΣαBt+BtΣαB˙t.\Sigma_t=B_t\Sigma_\alpha B_t, \qquad \dot\Sigma_t=\dot B_t\Sigma_\alpha B_t+B_t\Sigma_\alpha\dot B_t.Σt​=Bt​Σα​Bt​,Σ˙t​=B˙t​Σα​Bt​+Bt​Σα​B˙t​.
(84)

At equilibrium,

BΣβ−1B=Σα−1,q=mβ,B\Sigma_\beta^{-1}B=\Sigma_\alpha^{-1}, \qquad q=m_\beta,BΣβ−1​B=Σα−1​,q=mβ​,
(85)

which is equivalent to BΣαB=ΣβB\Sigma_\alpha B=\Sigma_\betaBΣα​B=Σβ​. The stationary map is therefore the Gaussian Brenier map, and the endpoint covariance is governed by the same Bures--Wasserstein geometry as in Section Entropic Optimal Transport Between Gaussians. This Gaussian reduction should be viewed as the finite-dimensional covariance shadow of the vanishing-temperature continuous Sinkhorn limit, not as the fixed-temperature Gaussian Sinkhorn formula itself.

In one dimension the flow reduces to

∂tut(x)=log⁡(1+ut′′(x))−G(x+ut′(x))+F(x)−rˉt,\partial_tu_t(x) = \log(1+u_t''(x))-G(x+u_t'(x))+F(x)-\bar r_t,∂t​ut​(x)=log(1+ut′′​(x))−G(x+ut′​(x))+F(x)−rˉt​,
(86)

as long as 1+ut′′>01+u_t''>01+ut′′​>0. This scalar case is useful for visualization because the potential curves can be plotted directly and the positivity condition is exactly the monotonicity of x↦x+ut′(x)x\mapsto x+u_t'(x)x↦x+ut′​(x).

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-continuous-epsilon-flow")

Continuous ε\varepsilonε-Sinkhorn flow in one dimension. The curves are snapshots of the gauge-fixed potential utu_tut​, initialized at u0=0u_0=0u0​=0, under the parabolic Monge--Ampere equation obtained from Berman’s high-resolution, vanishing-temperature Sinkhorn scaling. Time is encoded from red to blue; the faint bottom silhouettes show the source density in red and the target density in blue.

Interactive panel. Adjust the entropic scale and flow time to watch the log-domain continuous Sinkhorn relaxation approach the fixed-point dual potentials.

Sample Complexity¶

This section separates two statistical regimes. Exact OT resolves geometry at all spatial scales and pays rates controlled by the metric entropy, hence by the intrinsic dimension, of the support. Fixed-temperature Sinkhorn divergences smooth the dual potentials and recover parametric fluctuations, at the price of regularization bias.

The sample complexity of unregularized OT suffers from the curse of dimensionality, but the relevant dimension is geometric. If the distributions are supported on a regular lower-dimensional set, for instance a d′d'd′-dimensional submanifold of Rd\RR^dRd, the empirical rate is governed by d′d'd′ rather than by the ambient dimension ddd. In the high-dimensional regime for Wp\Wass_pWp​, namely d′>2pd'>2pd′>2p, this gives the characteristic rate n−1/d′n^{-1/d'}n−1/d′. Exact OT is therefore dimension-adaptive: it sees the intrinsic dimension of the data support through its covering numbers Dudley, 1969Weed & Bach, 2019. Entropic regularization changes a different aspect of the picture: for a fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, Sinkhorn divergences have parametric n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 statistical rates, although the constant deteriorates when ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0 Genevay et al., 2019Bigot et al., 2019. Related two-sample-testing viewpoints are developed in Ramdas et al., 2017, and the large-ϵ\epsilonϵ kernel limit connects to classical MMD tests Gretton et al., 2012.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-bias-variance-tradeoff")

Empirical fluctuations in dimensions three and six. For each sample size nnn, two independent empirical measures are drawn from the same standard Gaussian law. Exact OT follows a slower dimension-dependent scale, while MMD and the fixed-ϵ\epsilonϵ Sinkhorn divergence behave closer to the parametric n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 guide. This is a statistical illustration, not a solver benchmark.

Proposition: Empirical OT has Intrinsic-Dimension Value Rates

Let α\alphaα and β\betaβ be probability distributions with densities bounded above and below on [0,1]d[0,1]^d[0,1]d, and let α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ and β^m\hat\beta_mβ^​m​ be independent empirical measures. For d>2pd>2pd>2p, the expected empirical error for estimating the two-sample distance obeys

E∣Wp(α^n,β^m)−Wp(α,β)∣≲n−1/d+m−1/d.\mathbb E \abs{ \Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \Wass_p(\alpha,\beta) } \lesssim n^{-1/d}+m^{-1/d}.E∣∣​Wp​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Wp​(α,β)∣∣​≲n−1/d+m−1/d.
(87)

The exponent changes in low dimension, but the important message is that exact OT deteriorates with the intrinsic dimension of the support rather than necessarily with the ambient dimension. More generally, if the measures are supported on a compact set with regular d′d'd′-dimensional volume growth, so that covering numbers at scale rrr are of order r−d′r^{-d'}r−d′, the same estimate holds with ddd replaced by d′d'd′ when d′>2pd'>2pd′>2p. This includes smooth compact d′d'd′-dimensional submanifolds with densities bounded above and below with respect to their volume measure.

Proof Sketch

By the triangle inequality,

∣Wp(α^n,β^m)−Wp(α,β)∣≤Wp(α^n,α)+Wp(β^m,β).\abs{\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta)} \leq \Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha) + \Wass_p(\hat\beta_m,\beta).∣∣​Wp​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Wp​(α,β)∣∣​≤Wp​(α^n​,α)+Wp​(β^​m​,β).
(88)

For the one-sample term, partition [0,1]d[0,1]^d[0,1]d into dyadic cubes. At scale 2−j2^{-j}2−j, empirical mass fluctuations over the cells are of order n−1/22jd/2n^{-1/2}2^{jd/2}n−1/22jd/2, while moving this excess mass inside cells costs 2−j2^{-j}2−j. Summing the multiscale contributions up to the scale where the expected number of samples per cell is order one gives 2−J2^{-J}2−J with 2Jd≃n2^{Jd}\simeq n2Jd≃n, hence n−1/dn^{-1/d}n−1/d. On a support with d′d'd′-dimensional metric entropy, the same chaining argument uses covers with O(2jd′)O(2^{jd'})O(2jd′) cells at scale 2−j2^{-j}2−j, hence the balancing scale is 2Jd′≃n2^{Jd'}\simeq n2Jd′≃n and the rate is n−1/d′n^{-1/d'}n−1/d′. Matching lower bounds follow from packing arguments Dudley, 1969Fournier & Guillin, 2015Weed & Bach, 2019.

Proposition: MMD has a Parametric Value Rate

Let kkk be a bounded positive definite kernel with RKHS Hk\mathcal H_kHk​, and define

MMD⁡k(α,β):=∥∫k(x,⋅)  d(α−β)(x)∥Hk.\operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \norm{ \int k(x,\cdot)\,\d(\alpha-\beta)(x) }_{\mathcal H_k}.MMDk​(α,β):=∥∥​∫k(x,⋅)d(α−β)(x)∥∥​Hk​​.
(89)

If α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ and β^m\hat\beta_mβ^​m​ are independent empirical measures, then

E∣MMD⁡k(α^n,β^m)−MMD⁡k(α,β)∣≤κ(1n+1m)\mathbb E \abs{ \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) } \leq \kappa\left(\frac1{\sqrt n}+\frac1{\sqrt m}\right)E∣∣​MMDk​(α^n​,β^​m​)−MMDk​(α,β)∣∣​≤κ(n​1​+m​1​)
(90)

when k(x,x)≤κ2k(x,x)\leq\kappa^2k(x,x)≤κ2.

Proof

Let Φ(x)=k(x,⋅)\Phi(x)=k(x,\cdot)Φ(x)=k(x,⋅) be the feature map and mα=EΦ(X)m_\alpha=\mathbb E\Phi(X)mα​=EΦ(X). The reverse triangle inequality gives

∣MMD⁡k(α^n,β^m)−MMD⁡k(α,β)∣≤MMD⁡k(α^n,α)+MMD⁡k(β^m,β).\abs{ \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) } \leq \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha) + \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\beta_m,\beta).∣∣​MMDk​(α^n​,β^​m​)−MMDk​(α,β)∣∣​≤MMDk​(α^n​,α)+MMDk​(β^​m​,β).
(91)

Independence cancels cross terms after taking squared norms and expectation:

EMMD⁡k(α^n,α)2=1nE∥Φ(X)−mα∥Hk2=1n(Ek(X,X)−Ek(X,X′)).\mathbb E\operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^2 = \frac1n \mathbb E\norm{\Phi(X)-m_\alpha}_{\mathcal H_k}^2 = \frac1n \left( \mathbb E k(X,X)-\mathbb E k(X,X') \right).EMMDk​(α^n​,α)2=n1​E∥Φ(X)−mα​∥Hk​2​=n1​(Ek(X,X)−Ek(X,X′)).
(92)

Jensen’s inequality and k(x,x)≤κ2k(x,x)\leq\kappa^2k(x,x)≤κ2 give the displayed bound.

Proposition: Sinkhorn Divergences Interpolate the Rates

Assume α\alphaα and β\betaβ are supported in a compact subset of Rd\RR^dRd and the cost is smooth. For fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, debiased Sinkhorn divergences satisfy representative empirical bounds of the form

E∣L‾cϵ(α^n,β^m)−L‾cϵ(α,β)∣≤Cc,dϵ−d/2(1n+1m),\mathbb E \abs{ \overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) } \leq C_{c,d}\epsilon^{-d/2} \left( \frac1{\sqrt n}+\frac1{\sqrt m} \right),E∣∣​Lcϵ​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Lcϵ​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cc,d​ϵ−d/2(n​1​+m​1​),
(93)

up to constants and exponents depending on the precise smoothness class and support diameter. Thus regularization removes the n−1/dn^{-1/d}n−1/d curse for fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ, while the prefactor deteriorates as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0.

Proof Sketch

By the envelope theorem, the fluctuation of Lcϵ\mathcal L_c^\epsilonLcϵ​ with respect to its first marginal is controlled by the class of entropic dual potentials. The soft ccc-transform smooths these potentials at spatial scale ϵ\sqrt\epsilonϵ​ for a quadratic-type cost. Covering a bounded ddd-dimensional domain at this scale gives an effective complexity of order ϵ−d/2\epsilon^{-d/2}ϵ−d/2. Standard Rademacher or Dudley entropy bounds then give an empirical-process fluctuation of order ϵ−d/2/n\epsilon^{-d/2}/\sqrt nϵ−d/2/n​ for each marginal. Applying the same estimate to the three terms defining the debiased divergence gives the stated bound.

Remark: No free lunch when approximating exact OT

The parametric rate in Proposition Proposition: Sinkhorn Divergences Interpolate the Rates holds for fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ. If the goal is to approximate the unregularized OT value, one must also account for the regularization bias. In a typical bounded-cost finite-dimensional regime,

∣Lˉcϵ(α,β)−Lc(α,β)∣≤Cϵ,E∣Lˉcϵ(α^n,β^n)−Lˉcϵ(α,β)∣≤Cc,dϵ−d/2n−1/2.\abs{\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)-\MK_\c(\alpha,\beta)} \leq C\epsilon, \qquad \EE\abs{\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_n)-\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)} \leq C_{c,d}\epsilon^{-d/2}n^{-1/2}.∣∣​Lˉcϵ​(α,β)−Lc​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cϵ,E∣∣​Lˉcϵ​(α^n​,β^​n​)−Lˉcϵ​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cc,d​ϵ−d/2n−1/2.
(94)

Balancing the two terms gives ϵ≃n−1/(d+2)\epsilon\simeq n^{-1/(d+2)}ϵ≃n−1/(d+2) and total error of order n−1/(d+2)n^{-1/(d+2)}n−1/(d+2). Equivalently, target accuracy η\etaη requires choosing ϵ≃η\epsilon\simeq\etaϵ≃η and n≃η−(d+2)n\simeq\eta^{-(d+2)}n≃η−(d+2) samples under this bound. Thus entropic smoothing improves the statistical behavior at fixed scale, but approximating exact OT still forces a bias-variance tradeoff whose exponent deteriorates with dimension.

The interactive demo below is only a scaling guide: change the dimension to see the exact-OT exponent flatten, and change ϵ\epsilonϵ to move the Sinkhorn bias floor against its parametric fluctuation term.

Interactive panel. This exploratory panel is a scaling guide. Use dimension, sample size, and epsilon to compare statistical fluctuation with regularization bias.

Monotone Clearing Beyond Variational Sinkhorn¶

The preceding convergence mechanisms mostly used variational structure: Sinkhorn is alternating KL projection, coordinate ascent on a dual objective, or a contraction in a projective metric. There is another, more algebraic, convergence mechanism which keeps the scaling form but discards the existence of an objective. In Galichon’s equilibrium-flow viewpoint, and in related work on substitutability and inverse isotonicity, one studies nonlinear market-clearing equations whose Jacobian has a substitute sign structure Galichon & Jacquet, 2024Galichon et al., 2022. This is the nonlinear analogue of the classical theory of nonsingular M-matrices and M-functions Moré & Rheinboldt, 1973Plemmons, 1977. The relevance for OT is that Sinkhorn is the canonical scaling example, but the same monotone clearing proof also covers fixed-point equations that are not first-order conditions of any convex regularized transport problem.

Two-block clearing maps¶

Write the unknowns as two blocks z=(u,v)∈Rn×Rmz=(u,v)\in\RR^n\times\RR^mz=(u,v)∈Rn×Rm, where uuu and vvv will be signed log-scalings, and let

Q(z)=(Qα(u,v),Qβ(u,v))∈Rn×Rm.Q(z)=(Q^\alpha(u,v),Q^\beta(u,v))\in\RR^n\times\RR^m.Q(z)=(Qα(u,v),Qβ(u,v))∈Rn×Rm.
(95)

The parallel coordinate-clearing map TTT is defined by solving, for each coordinate,

Qℓ(Tℓ(z),z−ℓ)=0,1≤ℓ≤n+m.Q_\ell(T_\ell(z),z_{-\ell})=0, \qquad 1\leq \ell\leq n+m.Qℓ​(Tℓ​(z),z−ℓ​)=0,1≤ℓ≤n+m.
(96)

This is the Jacobi version: all coordinates are cleared against the old values of the other coordinates. In two-block scaling problems, all coordinates in uuu decouple when vvv is fixed, and all coordinates in vvv decouple when uuu is fixed. The more common alternating Sinkhorn sweep is the Gauss--Seidel composition of these two block clearings; the order argument below is stated for the parallel map to keep the notation short. The same construction extends to any finite number of blocks.

Definition: Z-functions and M-functions

Let D⊂RND\subset\RR^ND⊂RN be an order interval. A map Q:D→RNQ:D\to\RR^NQ:D→RN is diagonally isotone if Qℓ(zℓ,z−ℓ)Q_\ell(z_\ell,z_{-\ell})Qℓ​(zℓ​,z−ℓ​) is nondecreasing in zℓz_\ellzℓ​ for every ℓ\ellℓ. It is a Z-function if increasing the other coordinates cannot increase the ℓ\ellℓ-th component:

z−ℓ≤z−ℓ′⟹Qℓ(zℓ,z−ℓ)≥Qℓ(zℓ,z−ℓ′).z_{-\ell}\leq z'_{-\ell} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad Q_\ell(z_\ell,z_{-\ell})\geq Q_\ell(z_\ell,z'_{-\ell}).z−ℓ​≤z−ℓ′​⟹Qℓ​(zℓ​,z−ℓ​)≥Qℓ​(zℓ​,z−ℓ′​).
(97)

For a C1C^1C1 map this means ∂Qℓ/∂zk≤0\partial Q_\ell/\partial z_k\leq0∂Qℓ​/∂zk​≤0 for k≠ℓk\neq\ellk=ℓ. An M-function is a Z-function that is inverse isotone:

Q(z)≤Q(z′)⟹z≤z′.Q(z)\leq Q(z')\quad\Longrightarrow\quad z\leq z'.Q(z)≤Q(z′)⟹z≤z′.
(98)

A vector zzz is a subsolution if Q(z)≤0Q(z)\leq0Q(z)≤0, and a supersolution if Q(z)≥0Q(z)\geq0Q(z)≥0.

The M-function assumption says that cross-effects have the sign of substitutes, and that own effects dominate them strongly enough to prevent a global reversal of order. Galichon, Samuelson and Vernet formulate this idea through nonreversingness and unified gross substitutes; for single-valued maps this is the inverse-isotone structure used below. A degenerate M0M_0M0​-function keeps the same order structure but allows a null gauge direction, which is exactly what happens for balanced Sinkhorn before a potential normalization is imposed.

Theorem: Monotone convergence of coordinate clearing

Let D=[z‾,z‾]⊂RND=[\underline z,\overline z]\subset\RR^ND=[z​,z]⊂RN be a closed order interval, with z‾\underline zz​ a subsolution and z‾\overline zz a supersolution. Assume that Q:D→RNQ:D\to\RR^NQ:D→RN is continuous, diagonally isotone and an M-function. Assume also that, for every z∈Dz\in Dz∈D and every ℓ\ellℓ, the scalar clearing equation Qℓ(ξ,z−ℓ)=0Q_\ell(\xi,z_{-\ell})=0Qℓ​(ξ,z−ℓ​)=0 has a unique solution ξ∈[z‾ℓ,z‾ℓ]\xi\in[\underline z_\ell,\overline z_\ell]ξ∈[z​ℓ​,zℓ​]. Then Q(z)=0Q(z)=0Q(z)=0 has a unique solution z⋆∈Dz^\star\in Dz⋆∈D. The Jacobi coordinate-clearing iterates starting from any subsolution in DDD increase monotonically to z⋆z^\starz⋆, while those starting from any supersolution in DDD decrease monotonically to z⋆z^\starz⋆.

Proof

Let TTT be the coordinate-clearing map. If z∈Dz\in Dz∈D is a subsolution, then Qℓ(zℓ,z−ℓ)≤0=Qℓ(Tℓ(z),z−ℓ)Q_\ell(z_\ell,z_{-\ell})\leq0=Q_\ell(T_\ell(z),z_{-\ell})Qℓ​(zℓ​,z−ℓ​)≤0=Qℓ​(Tℓ​(z),z−ℓ​). Diagonal isotonicity and uniqueness of the scalar zero give zℓ≤Tℓ(z)z_\ell\leq T_\ell(z)zℓ​≤Tℓ​(z) for every ℓ\ellℓ, hence z≤T(z)z\leq T(z)z≤T(z). Since QQQ is a Z-function,

Qℓ(T(z))≤Qℓ(Tℓ(z),z−ℓ)=0,Q_\ell(T(z))\leq Q_\ell(T_\ell(z),z_{-\ell})=0,Qℓ​(T(z))≤Qℓ​(Tℓ​(z),z−ℓ​)=0,
(99)

so T(z)T(z)T(z) is again a subsolution. Since every subsolution lies below every supersolution by inverse isotonicity, T(z)≤z‾T(z)\leq\overline zT(z)≤z, and therefore T(z)∈DT(z)\in DT(z)∈D. The supersolution argument is the same with all inequalities reversed. The lower and upper iterates are thus monotone and trapped in the compact interval DDD. Their limits exist, and passing to the limit in Qℓ(zℓk+1,z−ℓk)=0Q_\ell(z_\ell^{k+1},z_{-\ell}^k)=0Qℓ​(zℓk+1​,z−ℓk​)=0 gives Q(z⋆)=0Q(z^\star)=0Q(z⋆)=0. If zzz and z′z'z′ were two zeros in DDD, inverse isotonicity applied in both directions gives z=z′z=z'z=z′.

Proposition: Smooth M-matrix certificate

Let D⊂RND\subset\RR^ND⊂RN be a convex order interval and let Q:D→RNQ:D\to\RR^NQ:D→RN be C1C^1C1. Assume that DQ(z)DQ(z)DQ(z) has nonpositive off-diagonal entries for every z∈Dz\in Dz∈D. If there exists λ≫0\lambda\gg0λ≫0 such that

λ⊤DQ(z)≫0for all z∈D,\lambda^\top DQ(z)\gg0 \qquad\text{for all }z\in D,λ⊤DQ(z)≫0for all z∈D,
(100)

or equivalently

λℓ∂Qℓ∂zℓ(z)>∑k≠ℓλk∣∂Qk∂zℓ(z)∣,1≤ℓ≤N,\lambda_\ell\frac{\partial Q_\ell}{\partial z_\ell}(z) > \sum_{k\neq \ell}\lambda_k \left|\frac{\partial Q_k}{\partial z_\ell}(z)\right|, \qquad 1\leq \ell\leq N,λℓ​∂zℓ​∂Qℓ​​(z)>k=ℓ∑​λk​∣∣​∂zℓ​∂Qk​​(z)∣∣​,1≤ℓ≤N,
(101)

then QQQ is an M-function on DDD.

Proof

The off-diagonal sign gives the Z-property by integrating the partial derivatives along coordinatewise increasing segments. The displayed inequalities imply that each DQ(z)DQ(z)DQ(z) is a strictly weighted column diagonally dominant Z-matrix, hence a nonsingular M-matrix; in particular its inverse is nonnegative for each fixed zzz Plemmons, 1977. For two points z,z′∈Dz,z'\in Dz,z′∈D, set

A=∫01DQ(z′+t(z−z′)) dt.A=\int_0^1 DQ\big(z'+t(z-z')\big)\,dt .A=∫01​DQ(z′+t(z−z′))dt.
(102)

The matrix AAA has the same Z-sign pattern and the same strict weighted diagonal dominance, so it is again a nonsingular M-matrix and A−1≥0A^{-1}\geq0A−1≥0. Since Q(z)−Q(z′)=A(z−z′)Q(z)-Q(z')=A(z-z')Q(z)−Q(z′)=A(z−z′), the implication Q(z)≤Q(z′)Q(z)\leq Q(z')Q(z)≤Q(z′) gives z−z′=A−1(Q(z)−Q(z′))≤0z-z'=A^{-1}(Q(z)-Q(z'))\leq0z−z′=A−1(Q(z)−Q(z′))≤0. This is inverse isotonicity. The positive diagonal entries also give diagonal isotonicity.

Sinkhorn as the canonical M0M_0M0​-system¶

Let K=exp⁡(−C/ϵ)>0K=\exp(-C/\epsilon)>0K=exp(−C/ϵ)>0 and write

Pij=riKijsj,ri=eui,sj=e−vj.P_{ij}=r_iK_{ij}s_j, \qquad r_i=e^{u_i},\qquad s_j=e^{-v_j}.Pij​=ri​Kij​sj​,ri​=eui​,sj​=e−vj​.
(103)

The signed convention v=−log⁡sv=-\log sv=−logs makes the clearing equations

Qiα(u,v)=∑jKijeui−vj−ai,Qjβ(u,v)=bj−∑iKijeui−vj.Q_i^\alpha(u,v)=\sum_jK_{ij}e^{u_i-v_j}-a_i, \qquad Q_j^\beta(u,v)=b_j-\sum_iK_{ij}e^{u_i-v_j}.Qiα​(u,v)=j∑​Kij​eui​−vj​−ai​,Qjβ​(u,v)=bj​−i∑​Kij​eui​−vj​.
(104)

Then Q=0Q=0Q=0 is exactly P1=aP\mathbf 1=aP1=a and P⊤1=bP^\top\mathbf 1=bP⊤1=b, and coordinate clearing gives the usual Sinkhorn scalings

ri+=ai∑jKijsj,sj+=bj∑iKijri.r_i^+=\frac{a_i}{\sum_jK_{ij}s_j}, \qquad s_j^+=\frac{b_j}{\sum_iK_{ij}r_i}.ri+​=∑j​Kij​sj​ai​​,sj+​=∑i​Kij​ri​bj​​.
(105)

The Jacobian has positive diagonal entries and nonpositive off-diagonal entries. Its column sums vanish, reflecting the gauge invariance (u,v)↦(u+c1,v+c1)(u,v)\mapsto(u+c\mathbf 1,v+c\mathbf 1)(u,v)↦(u+c1,v+c1). Thus balanced Sinkhorn is naturally an M0M_0M0​-system. After fixing a gauge, the reduced Jacobian is a graph-Laplacian minor; under connected support it is a nonsingular M-matrix. This complements the variational and Hilbert-metric proofs above, and also connects Sinkhorn scaling with choice models Qu et al., 2023.

Example: Lossy Sinkhorn clearing with outside options

The following OT-shaped clearing model is deliberately minimal. It keeps a positive transport kernel and multiplicative scalings, but introduces outside options and lossy arrivals. It should be read as a toy absorptive matching model rather than as a new transport distance. Let 0<ηij≤10<\eta_{ij}\leq10<ηij​≤1, σi>0\sigma_i>0σi​>0, τj>0\tau_j>0τj​>0, and set

Pij=riKijsj,ri=eui,sj=e−vj.P_{ij}=r_i\K_{ij}s_j,\qquad r_i=e^{u_i},\qquad s_j=e^{-v_j}.Pij​=ri​Kij​sj​,ri​=eui​,sj​=e−vj​.
(106)

The clearing equations are

σiri+∑jPij=ai,τjsj+∑iηijPij=bj.\sigma_i r_i+\sum_jP_{ij}=a_i, \qquad \tau_j s_j+\sum_i\eta_{ij}P_{ij}=b_j.σi​ri​+j∑​Pij​=ai​,τj​sj​+i∑​ηij​Pij​=bj​.
(107)

They say that source mass can exit through an outside sink, while target demand can be met either by effective arrivals or by a local outside source. The coordinate-clearing update is still Sinkhorn-like:

ri+=aiσi+∑jKijsj,sj+=bjτj+∑iηijKijri.r_i^+=\frac{a_i}{\sigma_i+\sum_j\K_{ij}s_j}, \qquad s_j^+=\frac{b_j}{\tau_j+\sum_i\eta_{ij}\K_{ij}r_i}.ri+​=σi​+∑j​Kij​sj​ai​​,sj+​=τj​+∑i​ηij​Kij​ri​bj​​.
(108)

In the variables (u,v)(u,v)(u,v), the off-diagonal derivatives again have the Z-sign. On the invariant domain ri≤ai/σir_i\leq a_i/\sigma_iri​≤ai​/σi​, the simple sufficient condition

τj>∑i(1−ηij)Kijaiσi,1≤j≤m,\tau_j>\sum_i(1-\eta_{ij})\K_{ij}\frac{a_i}{\sigma_i}, \qquad 1\leq j\leq m,τj​>i∑​(1−ηij​)Kij​σi​ai​​,1≤j≤m,
(109)

implies the weighted diagonal-dominance certificate 1⊤DQ≫0\ones^\top DQ\gg01⊤DQ≫0, hence Proposition Proposition: Smooth M-matrix certificate shows that the system is an M-function on that domain. Indeed, for the clearing map Qiα=σiri+∑jPij−aiQ_i^\alpha=\sigma_i r_i+\sum_jP_{ij}-a_iQiα​=σi​ri​+∑j​Pij​−ai​ and Qjβ=bj−τjsj−∑iηijPijQ_j^\beta=b_j-\tau_j s_j-\sum_i\eta_{ij}P_{ij}Qjβ​=bj​−τj​sj​−∑i​ηij​Pij​, the column sums of the Jacobian are

σiri+∑j(1−ηij)Pij>0,τjsj−∑i(1−ηij)Pij=sj(τj−∑i(1−ηij)Kijri)>0.\sigma_i r_i+\sum_j(1-\eta_{ij})P_{ij}>0, \qquad \tau_j s_j-\sum_i(1-\eta_{ij})P_{ij} = s_j\left(\tau_j-\sum_i(1-\eta_{ij})\K_{ij}r_i\right)>0.σi​ri​+j∑​(1−ηij​)Pij​>0,τj​sj​−i∑​(1−ηij​)Pij​=sj​(τj​−i∑​(1−ηij​)Kij​ri​)>0.
(110)

The model is generally non-variational. If Q=∇ΦQ=\nabla\PhiQ=∇Φ for a C2C^2C2 potential, the cross-partials would satisfy

∂Qiα∂vj=∂Qjβ∂ui.\frac{\partial Q_i^\alpha}{\partial v_j} = \frac{\partial Q_j^\beta}{\partial u_i}.∂vj​∂Qiα​​=∂ui​∂Qjβ​​.
(111)

Here this would force −Pij=−ηijPij-P_{ij}=-\eta_{ij}P_{ij}−Pij​=−ηij​Pij​ on every active arc, hence ηij=1\eta_{ij}=1ηij​=1. Lossy arrivals therefore destroy exactness of the one-form ∑ℓQℓ  dzℓ\sum_\ell Q_\ell\,\d z_\ell∑ℓ​Qℓ​dzℓ​, while preserving the monotone clearing structure.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-mfunctions-nonvariational-scaling")

Non-variational lossy Sinkhorn scaling on two Gaussian-mixture point clouds. Across columns, the outside-option scale ρ\rhoρ increases in σ\sigmaσ and τ\tauτ. Colors show centered log-scalings, log⁡r−⟨log⁡r⟩\log r-\langle\log r\ranglelogr−⟨logr⟩ on the source row and log⁡s−⟨log⁡s⟩\log s-\langle\log s\ranglelogs−⟨logs⟩ on the target row; faint violet links mark the largest entries of the induced effective plan. The first displayed case uses uniform outside coefficients, while the second uses spatially varying outside coefficients and directional loss factors. The updates are Sinkhorn-like row and column clearings, but ηij≠1\eta_{ij}\neq1ηij​=1 breaks the cross-partial symmetry required by a convex potential.

References¶
  1. Bregman, L. M. (1967). The relaxation method of finding the common point of convex sets and its application to the solution of problems in convex programming. USSR Computational Mathematics and Mathematical Physics, 7(3), 200–217.
  2. Fortet, R. (1940). Résolution d’un système d’équations de M. Schrödinger. Journal de Mathématiques Pures et Appliquées, 19(1–4), 83–105. https://www.numdam.org/item/JMPA_1940_9_19_1-4_83_0/
  3. Essid, M., & Pavon, M. (2019). Traversing the Schrödinger Bridge Strait: Robert Fortet’s Marvelous Proof Redux. Journal of Optimization Theory and Applications, 181, 23–60.
  4. Léonard, C. (2019). Revisiting Fortet’s proof of existence of a solution to the Schrödinger system. arXiv Preprint arXiv:1904.13211.
  5. Lemmens, B., & Nussbaum, R. (2012). Nonlinear Perron-Frobenius Theory (Vol. 189). Cambridge University Press.
  6. Peyré, G. (2026). Robust Sublinear Convergence Rates for Iterative Bregman Projections. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2602.01372.
  7. Altschuler, J., Weed, J., & Rigollet, P. (2017). Near-linear time approximation algorithms for optimal transport via Sinkhorn iteration. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 30, 1964–1974.
  8. Dvurechensky, P., Gasnikov, A., & Kroshnin, A. (2018). Computational Optimal Transport: Complexity by Accelerated Gradient Descent Is Better Than by Sinkhorn’s Algorithm. In J. Dy & A. Krause (Eds.), Proceedings of the 35th International Conference on Machine Learning (Vol. 80, pp. 1367–1376). PMLR.
  9. Genevay, A., Chizat, L., Bach, F., Cuturi, M., & Peyré, G. (2019). Sample Complexity of Sinkhorn Divergences. Proceedings of the Twenty-Second International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, 89, 1574–1583.
  10. Bigot, J., Cazelles, E., & Papadakis, N. (2019). Central limit theorems for entropy-regularized optimal transport on finite spaces and statistical applications. Electronic Journal of Statistics, 13(2), 5120–5150. 10.1214/19-EJS1637
  11. Franklin, J., & Lorenz, J. (1989). On the scaling of multidimensional matrices. Linear Algebra and Its Applications, 114, 717–735.
  12. Birkhoff, G. (1957). Extensions of Jentzsch’s theorem. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society, 85(1), 219–227.
  13. Samelson, H. (1957). On the Perron-Frobenius theorem. Michigan Mathematical Journal, 4(1), 57–59.
  14. Janati, H., Muzellec, B., Peyré, G., & Cuturi, M. (2020). Entropic Optimal Transport between Unbalanced Gaussian Measures has a Closed Form. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems.
  15. Chizat, L., Delalande, A., & Vaškevičius, T. (2024). Sharper Exponential Convergence Rates for Sinkhorn’s Algorithm in Continuous Settings. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2407.01202.
